supports the following wildcards in the greeting, which are replaced
with current values as the mission runs:

- <player> or <p> - callsign of the player
- <unit> or <u> - name of the unit that the player is
    controlling
- <type> - type (e.g. “A-10A”) of the unit that the player is
    controlling
- <group> or <g> - name of the group that the player’s unit is
    in
- <in> - number of greetings that the player has received
    from this valet
- <out> - number of goodbyes that the player has received
    from this valet
- <n> creates a line feed
- <z> - valet zone’s name as set with ME
- <t> - current mission time in the format “HH:MM:SS”, e.g.
    “08:42:11”. Format be further configured with the _timeFormat_
    attribute.
- <lat> - latitude of the valet zone’s current position
- <lon> - longitude of the valet zone’s current position
- <ele> - elevation of the valet zone’s current position.
    Format be further configured with the _imperialUnits_ attribute.
- <mgrs> - valet zone’s current position in MGRS coordinates
- <v: flagName> - value of flag <flagName>
- <t: flagName> - value of flag <flagName> interpreted as
    time. Format be further configured with the _timeFormat_
    attribute.
- <lat: unit/zone> - latitude of unit/zone with that name.
- <lon: unit/zone> - longitude of unit/zone with that
    name.
- <ele: unit/zone> - elevation of unit/zone with that
    name. Format be further configured with the _imperialUnits_
    attribute.
- <twn: unit/zone> outputs the name of the nearest
    named location on the map. Requires the twn module
- <loc: unit/zone> outputs position relative to nearest
    named map location. Requires twn module


**Name Description**

- <mgrs: unit/zone> - mgrs of unit/zone with that name
- <coa: flag/unit/zone> - faction (e.g., “RED”) of
    zone/unit, or the flag value interpreted as such.
- <vel: unit/zone> - velocity of unit/zone with that name.
    Format be further configured with the _imperialUnits_ attribute.
- <alt: unit/zone> - altitude of unit/zone with that name.
    Format be further configured with the _imperialUnits_ attribute.
- <hdg: unit/zone> - heading of unit/zone with that name.
- <type: unit> - type (e.g. “A-10A”) of unit with that name
- <player: unit> - player callsign who controls a unit with
    that name.

```
If the message text is empty, no message is displayed.
```
Defaults to <empty message>, i.e., no greeting diosplayed
firstGreeting When present, the text of this message replaces the message that
is displayed when a player enters the valet for the first time since
they (re)spawned. If the attribute is present but the text is empty, no
message is displayed (i.e., this can be used to suppress the first
greeting to a player)

```
Supports all wildcards that greeting does.
```
Defaults to <none, no separate first greeting)
goodbye Message to display when a player leaves the valet zone

```
Supports all wildcards that greeting does.
```
Defaults to <empty>, i.e. no text message.
imperial
imperialUnits

When set to true, wildcards return units for velocities, length etc. in
“imperial” funny units (knots, feet, tablespoon etc.)
Defaults to false (SI units are used: m, km/h)
timeFormat The time format in which time values are displayed.
Defaults to <:h>:<:m>:<:s>
duration Time duration (in seconds) that a text message is displayed for
Defaults to 30
inSoundFile Name of the sound file that is to be played when a player enters the
valet zone
Defaults to <none>
firstInSoundFile When present, this audio is played when a player enters the valet
for the first time since they (re)spawned.
Defaults to <none>, i.e., insoundFile is played
outSoundFile Name of the sound file that is to be played when a player leaves the
valet zone
Defaults to the value of _inSoundFile_
greetSpawns Controls if a unit that spawns inside a valet zone should be greeted.
If set to false, a unit that spawns within a valet zone will not receive
a greeting, and must exit and then re-enter the valet zone for its first
greeting.
Default is false (spawning units inside a valet zone will not be
greeted but have to leave zone first)
method DML method for outputs


**Name Description**
valetMethod Defaults to ‘inc’
hi! Output flag to set when an eligible player unit enters the valet zone
bye! Output flag to set when an eligible player unit leaves the valet zone
coalition
valetCoalition

When given, restricts the valet to players of that coalition (e.g.,
‘red’). Players of other coalitions are ignored.
Defaults to <none> - all players are eligible
types
valetTypes

```
A list of unit types (e.g., “A-10A”), separated by comma. The valet
zone is restricted to players who control units of that type. List
supports end-wildcards, i.e., when the type ends on an asterisk “*”,
all types that match everything before the asterisk are matches.
Example: ”A-10*” matches “A-10A”, “A-10C” and “A-10C_2”, but not
“AV8BNA”.
```
```
Types are not case sensitive.
```
Defaults to <none>, all types permitted
groups
valetGroups

```
A list of group names, separated by comma. The valet zone is
restricted to players who control units who are members of the
groups on the list. List supports end-wildcards, i.e., when the type
ends on an asterisk “*”, all group names that match everything
before the asterisk are matches. Example: ”atta*” matches
“Attackers-1” and “atta99” but not “avenger- 1 ”
```
```
Group names are NOT case sensitive.
```
Defaults to <none> - no group restrictions
units
valetUnits

```
A list of unit names, separated by comma. The valet zone is
restricted to players who control units with names on the list. List
supports end-wildcards, i.e., when the type ends on an asterisk “*”,
all unit names that match everything before the asterisk are
matches. Example: ”atta*” matches “Attackers-1” and “atta99” but
not “avenger-1”
```
```
Unit names are NOT case sensitive.
```
Defaults to <none> - no unit restrictions
groundOnly A unit only triggers valet if it is on the ground

```
Defaults to false (airbourne units also trigger)
```
_5.3.37.5 Using the module_
To use valets in your mission, add the script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action during
Mission Start

To use

- add the ‘valet’ attribute to a zone. Usually, you’d then also supply at least one of the
    attributes to define behavior for _greeting_ and _goodbye_.


## 5.3.38 flareZone

_5.3.38.1 Description_
Like _FireFX_ and _SmokeZone_ ,
FlareZone is a small, convenient
module to perform a single function
well. FlareZones launch flares from
their zone’s center. They are a lot
easier to use than vanilla ME, and
they offer significant Quality-of-Life
enhancements over pure ME: random
flare color, direction, duration and
multiple flare launches. Also,
FlareZones support salvos of flares
with a randomizable duration for the
salvo, all which can be helpful if you
want to create some fireworks
display.

**Color**
You can either choose one of the
standard DCS flare colors (red,
green, white, yellow).

**Launch Direction**
By default, all flares are launched towards North. You can change this by giving a compass
heading in degrees (e.g., 270).

**Salvo Size and Duration**
When triggered, a single flare is launched immediately. You can change this so that multiple
flares are launched over a user-selectable duration

**Randomization**
FlareZone supports the following randomizations:

- _Color_
- _Salvo Size:_ minimal and maximal number of flares to launch
- _Duration:_ minimal and maximal duration in seconds
- _Direction:_ lower and upper bounds of heading to launch the flare. Only positive
    headings are supported.

_5.3.38.2 Dependencies_
FlareZonet requires dcsCommon and cfxZones to run


_5.3.38.3 Module Configuration_
Currently, FlareZones do not use config zones.

_5.3.38.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
flare** Tells DML that this trigger zone is a flare zone.

```
The value of this attribute specifies the flare’s color. Valid values are
“green”, “red”, “white”, “yellow”, “random”, “rnd”, or “-1” (for random),
“0” (for green), “1” (for red), “2” (for white) or “3” (for yellow)
```
```
Defaults to “green”
```
**MANDATORY**
rndLoc If set to true, the flare is launched from a random location inside the
flare zone.

Defaults to false (flare is launched from center of the zone)
launch?
launchFlare?
f?

```
Watchflag. When the value of this flag changes, the flares are
launched. If no flag is defined, no flares can be launched.
```
Defaults to <none>
triggerMethod
flareTriggerMethod

DML Method by which the flag is triggered.
Defaults to “change”
direction Compass heading in degrees (or a range) in which to launch the
flare. The heading must be a positive number. When you supply a
range, the direction will be randomized between the lower and
upper bounds. Note that both numbers in the range must be positive
numbers, and the upper bound must be larger than the lower. So if
you want the flares to launch from 270 to 90 degrees, you need to
add 260 to the second number.

```
Examples:
```
- 45 (launch in NE direction)
- 90 - 180 (launch somewhere between East and South)
- 270 - 450 (launch between West and East)

Defaults to 0 (North)
altitude
flareAlt
agl

```
Height (in meters) above ground where the flare is launched from.
```
Defaults to 1 (m above ground)
salvo Number of flares to launch when the zone is triggered. Can be
range. If you supply a range, a random number is chosen inside the
range.

```
Examples:
```
- 3 (launch 3 flares
- 2 - 5 (launch from 2 to 5 flares)

Defaults to 1
duration Only applicable when salvo contains more than 1 flares.
The time (in seconds) over which the salvo is to be launched. If, for


**Name Description**
example salvo size is 4 flares, and duration is 2 seconds, a flare will
be launched every 2/4 = 0.5 seconds, so that all 4 flares are
launched of the span of 2 seconds.

```
Duration can be a range, in which case the duration is randomized
to a value inside the range.
```
```
Defaults to 1 (second)
```
_5.3.38.5 Using the module_
To use flareZones in your mission, add the script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME where you want the flare(s) to be launched
- Add the ‘flare’ and trigger attribute to that zone.
- Add other attributes to customize to your needs


## 5.3.39 playerZone

_5.3.39.1 Description_
Player zone is a utility module that counts the number of players currently inside the zone,
and that can generate events on the outputs when the number changes.

Additionally, it can set flags to the current number of players per faction, and the total
number of players currently in the game.

_5.3.39.2 Dependencies_
playerZone requires dcsCommon, cfxZones.

_5.3.39.3 Module Configuration_
To change playerZone’s global options,

- Create a Trigger Zone anywhere using ME
- Name it “playerZoneConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
red# Name of the flag(s) to set to the current number of players that belong
to the RED faction that are currently in the game. This value is updated
once per second
blue# Name of the flag(s) to set to the current number of players that belong
to the BLUE faction that are currently in the game. This value is
updated once per second
neutral# Name of the flag(s) to set to the current number of players that belong
to the NEUTRAL faction that are currently in the game. This value is
updated once per second
total# Name of the flag(s) to set to the current number of players (any faction)
that are currently in the game. This value is updated once per second

_5.3.39.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
playerZone** Tells DML that this trigger zone is a player zone.

```
The value of this attribute specifies which faction’s players are
counted. Possible values
```
- “0” or “neutral” – red and blue are counted


**Name Description**

- “1” or “red” – only red players are counted
- “2” or blue – only blue players are counted

```
Defaults to “neutral” (red and blue players are counted)
```
**MANDATORY**
method
pwMethod

DML output method.
Defaults to “inc”
pNum# Direct output: the number of qualifying player units that are inside
the zone at this moment.
Defaults to <none>
added! Output to bang! when players units have entered the zone since the
last time the zone checked. Note that there are edge cases where
added! and removed! can signal a change even though the flag
pNum remains the same: for example if at the same time one player
unit leaves the zone (or is killed), and another player unit enters the
zone.
Defaults to <none>
removed! Output to bang! when plyer units have left the zone since the last
time that the zone has checked. Note that there are edge cases
where added! and removed! can signal a change even though the
flag pNum remains the same: for example if at the same time one
player unit leaves the zone (or is killed), and another player unit
enters the zone.
Defaults to <none>

_5.3.39.5 Using the module_
To use playerZone in your mission, add the script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME in which players are counted
- Add ‘blue’ or ‘red’ as value if you only want to count players from that side
- Add other attributes according to your needs


## 5.3.40 TACAN

_5.3.40.1 Description_
Similar to the NDM module, this module allows you to place TACAN beacons on the map,
with all the bells and whistles you would expect from a DML module:

##### BASIC FUNCTIONALITY

In its most basic function, you simply place a trigger zone, and
add the TACAN attributes (channel, callsign, mode), and you are
set. A TACAN is placed at the center of the zone at mission start.

In-game a TACAN unit is placed on the ground, and any TACAN-
equipped plane can use the TACAN signal to home in on it and
perform approaches on any radial.

If the unit is destroyed, the TACAN signal ends, and the station can no longer be
approached, so destroying enemy TACANs can be viable mission goal (see ‘tacan tracking’
for more information).

##### DEPLOY / SHUT DOWN (DESTROY) ON DEMAND

TACANS from DML can deploy and shut down (disappear) on demand, controlled by flags.
When you tell a tacan zone to deploy their TACAN, it will usually first remove the last
TACAN that it spawned to simulate a re-supply/refreshment or (when the location is
randomized) relocation. Similarly, you can deactivate a deployed TACAN, which will destroy
the deployed TACAN unit. Note that only the last deployed TACAN unit will be destroyed
(see below when this can be an issue)

For advanced uses, removal before deployment can be disabled. In this case, new TACANs
can be deployed without their former ‘incarnation’ being destroyed. Be advised that similar to
cloners, when you tell a tacan zone to destroy the TACAN, only the last deployed TACAN is
destroyed, so when you disable destroy before deply (by setting preWipe to false) it is
possible that other active TACANs remain in the zone

##### RANDOMIZATION

The TACAN module supports heavy randomization: location, channel, callsign. For many
mission applications within the confines of DML, randomization of a TACAN’s properties can


be counter-productive, as a TACAN often is only useful if you know which channel it is
broadcasting on, and usually, they are placed in strategic locations (e.g., a runway, or to
guide incoming CAP onto a target location). Therefore, most of the randomization options
are primarily useful for mission designers who access the module via API. That being said,
all randomization capabilities are accessible to all mission designers.

##### ANNOUNCED CHANGES / COALITION

Tacan can announce the placement and decommissioning of TACANs during the mission.
This can be controlled individually per tacan zone.

##### LIST / DIRECTORY

The Tacan module can optionally provide a list per faction of known/available TACANs
placed on the map via TACAN zones. TACANs that are destroyed or removed no longer
appear on the list.

##### TACAN TRACKING

Since TACANS can be strategic targets, mission designers may want to track their
destruction with the Group Tracker module. Each tacan zone provides an easy interface to
pass a newly spawned TACAN unit to group trackers.

_5.3.40.2 Dependencies_
Tacan requires the modules dcsCommon and cfxZones.

Optionally: groupTracker

_5.3.40.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the tacan module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “tacanConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information. Default is false
list
GUI

```
Setting this attribute to ‘true’ adds a menu into communications →
Other → Available TACAN stations to all players that automatically lists
all TACANs deployed by neutral or their own faction. Only lists
TACANS placed via tacan zones.
When requesting a list, a player receives a list of all currently live
TACANS placed by their or the neutral faction.
Defaults to false (no tacan interface)
```
_5.3.40.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description**


**Name Description
tacan** Tells DML that this trigger zone is a tacan zone.

```
The value of this attribute specifies which faction owns the TACAN.
Possible values
```
- “0” or “neutral”
- “1” or “red”
- “2” or blue

```
Defaults to “neutral” (TACAN is owned by neutral faction, listed for
red and blue factions when TACAN GUI is enabled)
```
**MANDATORY**
channel The channel to use for the TACAN, e.g. “72”.
For randomization, supports lists and ranges, e.g. “12, 17, 42-47”
Defaults to “1” (The channel 1)
mode Either “X” or “Y”
Mode “Y” usually is reserved for airborne TACAN (currently not
supported with tacan zones)
Defaults to “X”
callsign Three-letter callsign for this TACAN station, e.g. “TCN”.
For randomization, supports lists, e.g. “TXN”, “ABC”, “XYZ”
Defaults to “TXN”
onStart When set to true (default) the TACAN is created on mission start. If
set to false, you must provide a “deploy?” attribute to be able to
deploy the TACAN while the mission is running.
Defaults to true (deploy on mission start)
heading Orientation (in degrees) of the physical unit that is placed in game
when the TACAN is deployed. Has no influence on the TACAN’s
function.
Defaults to 0 (oriented North)
rndLoc When set to true, the TACAN is placed in a random location inside
the trigger zone. Supports polygonal trigger zones. Otherwise, the
TACAN is placed at the exact center of the trigger zone.
Defaults to false (place at center of zone)
triggerMethod DML method that tells when an input should trigger.
Defaults to “change”
deploy? Watchflag that triggers on triggerMethod and then initiates a
deployment cycle. If preWipe is set to false, the previously deployed
TACAN remains, otherwise it is removed before the new TACAN
deploys.
Defaults to <none>
preWipe If set to true (default), the last deployed TACAN from this zone is
removed (if it exists) before a new TACAN is deployed.
Defaults to true (remove last spawn before deploy)
destroy? Watchflag that triggers on triggerMethod and then removes the last
previously deployed TACAN if it still exists.
Defauts to <none>
c# Output (direct). Is always set to the currently selected channel of the
last spawned TACAN. When that TACAN is destroyed via destroy?
Signal, the cannel is set to 0 (zero)
Defaults to <none>
announcer When set to true, any TACAN created with this tacan zone is
announced with callsign, channel and mode to all players (when


**Name Description**
owning faction of the TACAN is neutral) or all players of the same
faction that also owns the TACAN.
Note that TACANS that are created at mission startup (onStart =
true) are not announced.
Defaults to false (no announcement)
trackWith: When a TACAN is created, it is tracked with the groupTracker
modules that are listed as value. Supports lists.
Note that the groupTracker module must load before the tacan
module in order to work correctly.
Defaults to <none>
desc (cosmetic only) – description to appear when this tacan is listed in
the TACAN GUI. Requires that GUI is active
Defaults to <none> (no additional description)

_5.3.40.5 Using the module_
To use the tacan module in your mission, add the script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT
action during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME
- Add a ‘tacan’ attribute and optionally set blue or red as owner.
- Add other attributes according to your needs (recommended: channel and callsign


## 5.3.41 (Simple) Mission Restart

_5.3.41.1 Description_
Restarting a mission is a relatively simple affair in DCS, provided that you do not change the
mission’s file name later. If you do (as many mission developers tend to do, for example to
include the version number), it becomes quite tedious. missionRestart is a tiny script that you
can add to your mission that restarts the mission when you change the value of the flag
named “simpleMissionRestart” to anything other than 0 (zero), no matter how often you
changed the mission’s file name.

That’s it. So, if you want to restart the mission at any time, simply change the flag
“simpleMissionRestart”, and the mission restarts.

**NOTE:**
Current implementations of DCS require that any mission that uses this script runs as
multiplayer, even when designed as a single-player version. It’s expected that in the near
future DCS runs all missions as multiplayer by default, as there are few downsides to
running any mission as “local multiplayer”, and performance is usually better, especially for
AI-heavy missions. So, you might start yourself right now

_5.3.41.2 Dependencies_
(Simple) missionRestart has no dependencies.

_5.3.41.3 Module Configuration_
(Simple) missionRestart requires no module configuration.

_5.3.41.4 ME Attributes_
(none)

_5.3.41.5 Using the module_
Run the script during mission start-up as a DOSCRIPT action.

Then, when it is time to restart, change the flag ‘simpleMissionRestart’ to any other value
than zero.


## 5.3.42 airfield

_5.3.42.1 Description_
The airfield module allows mission designers to query faction ownership of airfields/FARPs,
generate signals when the airfields/FARPs are conquered, and they allow mission designers
to arbitrarily force ownership of an airfield/FARP to a faction (for example, you can hand over
ownership of Senaki-Kolkhi). This module is especially helpful when you need cloners on
airfields to spawn clones that automatically belong to the airfield-owning faction, or when you
want to force ownership of an airfield (or FARP) after some event without having to also
place the required ‘boots’ on the ground first.

**BASIC FUNCTIONALITY: Reporting Ownership/Change in Ownership**
For some mission purposes you need a **signal/event when an airfield changes** hands (i.e.
is conquered by another faction), or you may need the currently owning faction of an airfield
as an input (for example for cloners). Airfield Zones attach themselves to (“associate with”)
the nearest airfield (or, optionally, airfields and FARPs), and they provide outputs that
generate a signal when red or blue conquer it with the red! and blue! outputs. If you have
an airfield and FARP in close proximity, make sure to verify that the airfield zone associates
itself with the airfield/FARP that you intended (by enabling verbosity during testing).

An output ownedBy# always reports the currently owning faction of the associated
airfield/FARP. You can use this direct output for a variety of purposes to easily implement
otherwise difficult uses: for example, to trigger something each time that the airfield changes
hands, or count how often an airfield has changed hands.

Also, **airfield zones always assume the same owner as the airfield/FARP that they
associate with**. Modules that can align themselves with zone ownership (e.g. cloners,
spawners) can use this to their advantage.

##### VISUAL CUES

Airfields can mark an airfield’s capture zone (the 2km circular zone emanating from the
airfield’s center in the color of your choosing (usually defaults to red/bue/grey). You can
optionally extend this ability to all airfields on your map, or only those that have an ‘airfield’
zone attached.

##### SETTING AIRFIELD OWNERSHIP

Airfield zones can force (set) ownership of the associated airfield/FARP at any time. For this
you can use the makeRed? and makeBlue? inputs, or use the ‘fixed’ attribute. The “fixed”
attribute forces ownership of the associated airfield/FARP at mission start to the coalition
you list, and then disables capture until a signal is received on the autoCap? input, which
revokes its ‘unconquerable’ status.

If the zone receives a signal on the makeRed? or makeBlue? inputs, it first wrests
ownership control from the mission (meaning that the airfield/FARP can no longer be
captured by ‘ground rules’) and then gives ownership to the respective faction. Ownership
remains with that faction until


- a different makeXXX? signal is received
- an autoCap? signal is received

If the zone receives an autoCap? signal, the associated airfield reverts to standard mission
capture “ground rules”.

If you want to inhibit an airfield’s/FARP’s capture from the start of the mission until you
decide otherwise, you can add a directControl = true attribute to the airfield zone.
Such a zone can’t be captured normally until it receives an autoCap? signal

If an airfield is captured by sending a signal on a makeXXX? input, a capture signal is sent
on the corresponding xxx! Output, but only if the airfield belonged to a different faction
before.

##### PERSISTENCE SUPPORT

airfield zones fully support persistence.

_5.3.42.2 Dependencies_
Airfield requires the modules dcsCommon and cfxZones.

If you are using persistence and cloners or spawners that take their ownership from airfield
zones, airfield should load before cloneZones and spawn zones.

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “airfieldConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

Alternatively, you can use the #RRGGBBAA format, where RR, GG, BB
and AA are each hexadecimals (as is used in HTML/CSS to denote

_5.3.42.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
airfield** Tells DML that this trigger zone should associate with the closest
airfield (or FARP, see config settings)

```
The owner of this zone is always the same as the owner of the
airfield/FARP that an airfield zone associates with.
```

**Name Description**

```
The value of this attribute is ignored.
```
**MANDATORY**
farps When set to true, airfield zones also associate with FARPs. When
set to false, FARPs are ignored.
When enabled, care must be taken when placing FARPS close to
airfields: an airfield zone associates itself with the _closest airfield or
FARP_ , and the in-game location of an airfield may not be where you
assume it is. Hint: enable verbosity and see which airfield zone
attaches to which object.

Defaults to false (associate only with airfields, disregard FARPs)
fixed Defines which side holds the associated airfield/FARP **at mission
start.** The airfield/FARP is made **unconquerable** , and remains in
that coalition’s possession until the zone receives a signal on one of
the following inputs:

- makeRed? – ownership is turned over to RED
- makeBLUE? – ownership is turned over to BLUE
- makeNeutral? – ownership is turned over to NEUTRAL
- autoCap? – makes the airfield/FARP capturable by mission
    ground capture rules.

```
Valid values are 0, 1, 2, red, blue, neutral
```
Defaults to <none>, airfield can be captured normally, and initial
ownership is as set up by mission editor.
method Output method for all outputs.
Defaults to ‘inc’
triggerMethod DML input method for all inputs
Defaults to ‘change’
red! Flags that should be sent a signal when the associated
airfield/FARP is captured by RED faction
Defaults to <none>
blue! Flags that should be sent a signal when the associated
airfield/FARP is captured by BLUE faction
Defaults to <none>
makeRed? When a signal is received on the flag that connects to this input, the
zone takes control of airfield/FARP ownership (no longer mission
ground capture rules), and forces ownership to RED faction.
If the airfield/FARP was previously owned by a different faction, a
signal is sent to red! Output.
To have the airfield return to being able to be captured by other
factions, send a signal to the autoCap? input.
Defaults to <none>
makeBlue? When a signal is received on the flag that connects to this input, the
zone takes control of airfield/FARP ownership (no longer mission

If the airfield/FARP was previously owned by a different faction, a

_5.3.42.5 Using the module_
To use the airfield module in your mission, add the script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT
action during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME close to the airfield (or FARP) that you want to associate
    the zone with (to control FARPS, the config zone must be set up accordingly)
- Add an ‘airfield’ attribute
- Add other attributes according to your requirements


## 5.3.43 ownAll

_5.3.43.1 Description_
ownAll is a coordination/helper module that pulls together, and consolidates, information
from multiple modules that deal with zone ownership - which is usually a major ingredient in
deciding win conditions. In short, it provides ‘ownership overview’ across multiple zones that
include

- Owned zones
- FARP Zones
- Airfield Zones
- ownAll (for cascades)

Use ownAll zones whenever you want to be informed when a faction owns a specific set of
zones.

##### BASIC FUNCTIONALITY

To set up, you give an ownAll zone a list of zones that it monitors. Given this set of zones,
an ownAll zones continuously gives you a tally of how many of these zones are owned by
red, and how many are owned by blue.

Should one faction manage to own all zones in the list, ownAll can give a signal on the
output for that particular faction.

Importantly, should one faction assume ownership of all zones that it monitors, **it sets it’s
own owner to that faction** , allowing you to cascade ownAll zones into ownership
hierarchies.

Compared to OwnedZones or other module that manage zone ownership, ownAll has a
number of important abilities:

- _Set-based_
    each ownAll zone operates on a set of zones that you, the mission designer, defines
    OwnedZones always regards _all_ owned zones over the entire map.
- _Cascading_
    ownAll zones will take on the ownership of whoever owns all zones listed in their set.
    This you can use to set up cascading groups of zones that can/must be captured by
    referencing other ownAll zones in the list of zones to monitor.
- _Module-agnostic_
    Since ownAll uses sets/lists of zones, you can use ownAll to pull together other
    zones with managed ownership independent of their type: they can be ownedZones,
    FARP Zones, Airfield Zones

_5.3.43.2 Dependencies_
ownAll requires the modules dcsCommon and cfxZones.


_5.3.43.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the ownAll module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “ownAllConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone (currently none defined)

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.
Defaults to false
redLine Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to draw the zone’s outline when it belongs to the RED faction.
RGBA values each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “1.0, 0, 0, 1.0”
redFill Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to fill the zone when it belongs to the RED faction. RGBA values
each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “1.0, 0, 0, 0.2”
blueLine Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to draw the zone’s outline when it belongs to the BLUE faction.
RGBA values each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “0, 0, 1.0, 1.0”
blueFill Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to fill the zone when it belongs to the BLUE faction. RGBA values
each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “0, 0, 1.0, 0.2”
neutralLine Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to draw the zone’s outline when it belongs to the NEUTRAL
faction. RGBA values each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 1.0”
neutralFill Four numeric values, separated by comma (e.g., 1.0, 0, 0, 1.0) that
define the RGBA (red, green, blue, alpha = opacity) values for the color
used to fill the zone when it belongs to the NEUTRAL faction. RGBA
values each range from 0.0 to 1.0
Defaults to “0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 0.2”

color used to draw the zone’s outline when it belongs to the RED
faction. RGBA values each range from 0.0 to 1.0

_5.3.43.5 Using the module_
To use the airfield module in your mission, add the module’s script to the mission as a
DOSCRIPT action during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME
- Add an ‘ownAll’ attribute and list the names of the trigger zones that should be
    monitored as value, separated by comma “(“,”)
- Add other attributes as required.


### 5.3.44 groundExplosion

_5.3.44.1 Description_
GroundExplosion is basically DML’s take on ME’s ‘EXPLODE’ trigger rule action, except that
it greatly expands on the concept, and fully integrates with DML’s concept of communication
with flags.

**BASIC FUNCTION**
With groundExplosions you can use other modules to trigger explosions on the ground or
near the ground (‘Flak’). When the module is triggered via the “boom?” input, it creates

- a single or series of explosions
- of a controllable strength
- at the center ort randomized location inside the trigger zone
- at controllable altitude
- (if multiple explosions are selected) over a customizable duration

Using these attributes, you can use the module to

- Destroy map/scenery objects at will
- Create (dangerous!) “Flak” – a series of explosions inside a volume over time
- Simulate bombardments on the ground
- Tightly controlled special effects for mission ‘flavor’

##### NUMBER OF EXPLOSIONS

The “num” attribute controls how many explosions the module will create each time that it is
triggered. If, for example, you supply “3” as value, the module will create three explosions
every time that ‘boom?’ is triggered. “nun” supports randomized ranges, meaning that if you
supply the range “3-7” for “num”, each time that “boom?” is triggered, the module picks a
number between 3 and 7 (inclusive) and then creates that many explosions.


##### STRENGTH

You control the strength of the explosion through a value added to the “explosion” attribute
itself. 1 is a fairly weak explosion, while 3000 usually is sufficient to level most buildings (and
their neighbors). groundExplosion supports randomization of explosion strength: if you
supply a range (e.g., “500-750”), the strength will be randomized to a value from that range
for every explosion created.

##### RANDOMIZED LOCATION

When “num” (the number of explosions to create) is set to any value larger than 1, or if you
set the “rndLoc” attribute to true, the location(s) of explosion are randomized to points inside
the trigger zone. Circular and Quad-based trigger zones are supported.

##### ALTITUDE

Although named “groundExplosion”, you can control the altitude at which the explosions
occur. The number that you specify here is always AGL in meters, and the attribute supports
ranges. In combination with the ‘num’ attribute, you can use this to quickly and easily create
a volume filled with explosions that is visually indistinguishable from WWII ‘Flak’ – and which
is also very dangerous to any aircraft within the volume.

**DURATION (multiple explosions only)**
Whenever you want more than one explosion to occur inside the trigger zone, you can also
set a time period over which these will occur, for example you can set up 6 explosions to
occur over the span of 2 seconds.

##### FLARES

Some explosions (e.g. munitions that are cooking off) have also flares erupting. If you want
this additional eye candy (it’s only a visual effect), you can add the ‘flares’ attribute. As a
value you give the number of flares that each explosion should set off. flares supports a
range, which will randomize the number of flares given off by each explosion to a value
between those two numbers.

_5.3.44.2 Dependencies_
groundExplosion requires the modules _dcsCommon_ and _cfxZones_.

_5.3.44.3 Module Configuration_
groundExplosion currently has no global settings and does not require a config zone.

_5.3.44.4 ME Attributes_

**Name Description
explosion** Marks this zone as an explosion zone. All explosions triggered will
occur within the zone (although their damage/blast may well extend


**Name Description**
past the zone.

```
The value of this attribute determines the strength of the explosion.
A value of 1 may be harmful to individual units, while a value of
3000 is almost sure to level a building (and others close by,
depending on their strength)
```
```
Supports ranges, so if you specify a strength of 50-230, each
individual explosion will receive its own randomized strength within
that range
```
```
Defaults to 1
```
**MANDATORY**
triggerMethod DML trigger method for inputs.
Defaults to “change”
boom? DML watchflag. When triggered, the module runs through an
explosion cycle, setting of as many explosions as specified by the
attributes

Defaults to <none>
num Number of explosions to perform every time that the input is
triggered.

```
Supports ranges, so setting num to “3-9” will cause
groundExplosion to set off a random number between 3 and 9
(inclusive) each time that the input is triggered.
```
Defaults to 1 (a single explosion)
rndLoc Controls where the explosion will be located. If set to false (default)
the explosion will happen at the exact center of the trigger zone.

```
If rndLoc is set to true (or num is greater than 1), the positions of all
explosions are always randomized to occur within the area of the
trigger zone.
```
```
NOTE:
If num is set to a value greater than one (1), the location of each
explosion is always randomized inside the zone.
```
Defaults to false (if a single explosion is chosen, it occurs at center
of zone)
agl Height (in meters) above local ground where the explosion occurs.
This allows you to create air bursts, and even Flak effects.

```
Important:
if your target zone spans large areas with steep inclines, this can
result in explosions with great variance in height, as the explosion
always occurs agl meters above the local ground.
```
```
Can be set to a random range (e.g. 20-30) that is applied to each
explosion individually
```

**Name Description**
Defaults to 1 (m above ground)
duration Only applicable if set to multiple explosions. Sets the time frame (in
seconds) for all explosions to occur within, after being triggered. So
if you set _num_ to 8 and _duration_ to 2, all 8 explosions occur within a
span of 2 seconds.

Defaults to 0 (all explosions happen immediately at time of
triggering)
flares Adds flares that erupt from the explosion. The value that you supply
controls the number of flares that are launched with each explosion,
and supports ranges (e.g. “1-3”).

```
Note that flares are always launched from the ground, so this effect
may look silly when used with agl.
Defaults to <none> - no flares
```
_5.3.44.5 Using the module_
To use this module in your mission, add its script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME
- Add an ‘ _explosion’_ and a ‘ _boom?_ ’ attribute, then
- add other attributes as required.
- The explosions are triggered with the flag that is connected to the _blow?_ input


### 5.3.45 Sweeper

_5.3.45.1 Description_
In DCS, stuff blows up. And sometimes, some of that stuff can obstruct runways. In most
missions, this isn’t much of a problem, but in rare instances it can lead to issues when they
obstruct key/choke points that AI units have to navigate. Missions that are long-running are
more susceptible than short. If wayward units, debris, burnt-out husks, even AI aircraft
obstruct progress of your mission, use Sweeper to ensure that your choke points are cleared
out regularly. There usually are only a few locations in your mission that must be swept, and
sweeping is a process that can be done with large (multi-minute) gaps in-between.

So, when you test your mission design, and you notice that units (e.g. AI flights) are queuing
up instead of being on their way, chances are good that something obstructs their path. For
example, a SAM site erected to protect an airfield may be attacked by the enemy, and the
SAM site units disperse. Since AI in DCS isn’t, one or more of these units may come to rest
on a runway, blissfully unaware of the fact that it now prevents all AI departures and landings
at that airfield. Sweeper can remedy this for you.

**How Sweeper Works**
Using sweeper is simple: set up a sweeper trigger zone, and any unit that remains inside
that zone longer than the sweeper interval is removed from the game. That’s all. Simply
being inside a sweeper zone isn’t an issue for sweeper. Being inside the zone longer than
the sweep interval will cause sweeper to remove it.

Above image shows how a trigger zones “Moz Sweep” placed over Mozdok’s runway
protects the runway from becoming inoperable should objects fall on the runway. Example
taken from the “Expansion” mission.

_5.3.45.2 Dependencies_
Sweeper requires _dcsCommon_ and _cfxZones_.

_5.3.45.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the sweeper module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “sweeperConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone (currently none defined)


**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.
Defaults to false
interval Time (in second) between to sweeper checks of a zone. Any unit that is
found inside the zone in two consecutive checks is immediately
removed from the game. Note that these checks can be costly
performance-wise, so multi-minute intervals are to be preferred.
Note also that if your mission has units traverse these zones, make
sure that the interval is large enough that the traversing units have time
to clear the zone (for example, some taxiway-less airfields can force
aircraft to backtaxi for take-off – a process that can easily take multiple
minutes)

```
Defaults to 300 Seconds (5 Minutes)
```
_5.3.45.4 ME Attributes_

**Name Description
sweeper** Marks this zone as a sweeper zone. No unit may remain longer than
interval (see above) inside the zone or it risks being removed from
the game

**MANDATORY**
aircraft If set to true, aircraft are included in the sweep and removed after
being found inside the zone on two consecutive checks

Defaults to true (aircraft are included in sweeps)
helos If set to true, helicopters are included in the sweep and removed
after being found inside the zone on two consecutive checks

```
Defaults to true (helicopters are included in sweeps)
```
_5.3.45.5 Using the module_
To use this module in your mission, add its script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME and place it over a choke point that must regularly be
    cleared of obstructions
- Add a ‘ _sweeper’_ attribute
- add other attributes as required.


### 5.3.46 twn (automatic Town locale bridge)

_5.3.46.1 Description_
Each map in DCS comes with it’s own internal data base of location information (mainly town
names and location) that was originally intended to be accessed by the Ka-50 (Black Shark)
Module. No other DCS module currently accesses this information, and sadly it is not
accessible through Mission Editor. But it comes with every map.

Using some black scripting magic and advanced code heresy, DML is now able to tap into
this information source and allow other DML modules to access it.

_5.3.46.2 Dependencies_
None. This module uses magic.

_5.3.46.3 Modules that automagically use twn when present_
Some modules automatically look for, and if present, automatically integrate with twn’s locale
information, e.g.

- csarManager
- convoy
- recon mode
- messenger’s “twn” and “loc” wildcard
- valet’s “twn” and “loc” wildcards
- reaper

_5.3.46.4 Module Configuration_
None

_5.3.46.5 ME Attributes_
None

_5.3.46.6 Using the module_
Add the twn module to your miz.


### 5.3.47 Convoy

_5.3.47.1 Description_
This module allows you to easily create (helicopter-escorted) convoys that you can send
across the map and receive signals when they arrive or are destroyed. The module also
supports reports for active (friendly and enemy) convoys.

**BUILDING A CONVOY: Ground and Helicopters**

In this regard convoys function very similar to cloners: you build ground troops (of your own
composition), place at least one unit inside the convoy zone, and define the route that they
should take. Also set their route orders (ROE, Alarm state etc.). You can place multiple
groups inside a convoy zone (with different compositions and routes) – and they are all
added to the group of potential convoys that are to be sent out.

Next, if you want helicopters to escort the convoy, add one or more helicopter groups into
the convoy zone and set up their armament and fuel.

When you activate the convoy, one of the ground groups and one of the helicopter groups
are randomly chosen. The helicopters are always set to escort the ground group and will
patrol around the moving convoy until they reach their goal.

##### DESTINATION AND COMPOSITION

A convoy is simply a group of ground units, and you assemble a convoy in Mission Editor the
same that you would any other ground group: choose the number and type of vehicles, then
assign a path to the group and define any options (ROE, alert stage etc.) that governs their
behavior.

Note that since you can have multiple groups inside a convoy zone, and groups can have
different destinations, a convoy spawned from one convoy source can have different
locations, depending on which group is chosen to spawn (a chosen group’s route/destination
is never changed). So, if you have three convoys sitting in a convoy zone, named “A” and
set up to go to WPA, “B” bound for WPB, and “C” driving to WPC), each time that a new
convoy is spawned, the module chooses one group: “A”, “B” or “C”. By choosing a group, the


destination is set: if “C” is chosen, that convoy always heads to its WPC. In other words,
routes are not exchanged between convoys.

Be advised that it doesn’t matter which coalition you choose inside ME to assemble a
convoy group. The coalition that they belong to is entirely determined by the coalition of the
convoy zone (see _coalition_ attribute). For simplicity, I recommend that you use one of the
combined joint task forces as source, so you gain access to all vehicles (changing countries
while assembling a group is very, very buggy in Mission Editor).

##### ONLY GROUND UNITS RELEVANT

Although from a player’s perspective a convoy can be a mix of ground vehicles and
helicopter, for DML only the ground units are relevant. If a ground vehicle is destroyed, a
signal can be sent on the _attacked!_ output. If an escorting helicopter is destroyed, no signal
is sent. Likewise, a convoy is considered dead when all ground vehicles are destroyed,
irrespective of how many helicopters remain.

##### STARTING A CONVOY

A convoy is started when the zone receives a signal on the _spawn?_ input. When a convoy
zone starts a new convoy, it

- randomly chooses one of the ground vehicle groups and spawns the group into the
    game. If _preWipe_ is true, any previous incarnations of this convoy, including their
    helicopter escorts, are de-spawned.
- When a ground vehicle group is chosen, its route is analyzed to determine the
    destination. If the _twn_ module is present, the nearest location’s name is retrieved,
    otherwise the name of the nearest trigger zone or airfield (whichever is closest to the
    last waypoint of the ground group’s route)
- if there are Helicopter groups defined, determines if it should add a helicopter escort
    by rolling the dice and comparing the result with _pEscort_
- If a helicopter escort is to be added, one of the helicopter groups is chosen at
    random, and their task is to aggressively defend the ground vehicles. The helicopters
    start in the air.

##### REPORTS AND GUI

The convoy module can provide its own menu to players that allows them to request status
information of convoys. Additionally, it can provide real-time updates when a convoy reaches
a waypoint, is under attack, or is destroyed. Mission designers can control what information
is provided in-mission with convoy’s config zone, and who receives this information.

**Real-Time messages**
When enabled, players are told when a convoy

- spawns:
    Convoy name (e.g. “Blue Convoy-76544”) where it originates from (if twn is present
    with information about the locale), where it is headed (if twn is present, again with
    locale information) and the distance is is going to travel, e.g.


```
When enabled, the enemy coalition receives an abridged version of this notification.
```
- reaches a waypoint (unless it is the first or last Waypoint):
    Convoy name with destination (with locale information if twn is loaded), and which
    waypoint was reached out of the total number of waypoints. Waypoint 1 is the spawn
    point, the last waypoint is the destination, e.g.

```
This information is never shown to enemy coalition
```
- reaches its destination
    Convoy name and locale if twn is active.
- is attacked (one or more units lost), if enabled
    Convoy name, destination (with locale if twn is loaded), location of attack (if twn is
    present)

```
(Note: only one such attack warning is sent every 5 minutes, so if that convoy
sustains more losses during the intervening time, no additional notices are sent.
```
- is destroyed:
    Convoy name and destination.

Note that players can also receive information about enemy convoys if that is enabled.
However, in this the information is anonymized as DML assumes that that information isn’t
available:

- On spawn:
    An anonymized convoy name (e,g, “CVY-3”), no origin and a generalized destination
    (the destination is derived from the name of the nearest trigger zone name or
    airfield/FARP, whichever is closest).
- On Arrival
    Anonymized convoy name and generalized destination.

**Reports**
When enabled, players have UI access to intelligence/field reports on the status of known
convoys. These reports can be requested from the Communication→Other... menu. When
requested, the report lists and groups convoys be faction, with the mission designer being
able to control if a faction can see information on enemy convoys through the config zone.


Enemy convoys are anonymized, with two important QoL additions to better implement
‘convoy hunter’ style missions:

If a side manages to destroy one or more ground units from an enemy convoy, that convoy
will list the number of remaining units as it is assumed that the attacking unit(s) reported in
their sighting.

If a convoy has reached the last leg of its route, that convoy’s information adds the line
“CLOSE TO DESTINATION” to alert players to the fact that this convoy may warrant higher
prioritization.

##### DESTINATIONS

Since convoys derive their destination name in some notifications and reports from the name
of the airfield/trigger zone nearest to the convoy’s final waypoint, you may want to take
advantage of this and place an (otherwise empty) trigger zone close to the last waypoint and
give that trigger zone a fitting name (e.g. “Poti Defense Bunkers”)

If the _twn_ module is active, the destination information can be significantly amended by
locale information.

##### ARRIVING AT DESTINATION

When a convoy reaches its last waypoint, it is considered as reaching its destination. In this
case,

- a notification (text) is sent out
- the flag that connects to the output _arrived!_ is triggered
- if the convoy zone’s _endWipe_ attribute is set, the convoy and escort are removed
    from the game.

##### OUTPUTS (SIGNALS)

- **attacked!**
    The convoy module periodically (every 10 seconds) checks all convoys. If it detects
    that a convoy has lost one or more ground units since the last check, and still has
    one or more units alive, it sends a signal on all flags that connect to the attacked!
    output. Note that the status of any escorting helicopter is ignored.
- **dead!**
    If the convoy module detect that all ground units (helicopters are ignored) of a convoy
    are destroyed, it sends a signal to all flags that connect to the _dead!_ output.
- **arrived!**
    When DCS deems that the ground vehicle group for the convoy has arrived at their


```
final waypoint, the convoy module sends a signal on the arrived! output to all flags
that connect to it.
```
_5.3.47.2 Dependencies_
Convoy requires _dcsCommon_ , _cfxZones_ and _cfxMX_.

Optional: _radioMenu_ (when using the _“attachTo:”_ attribute)

Convoy automatically integrates with _twn_ if it is present in the mission

_5.3.47.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the Convoy module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “convoyConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.

Defaults to false
actionSound Name of the soundfile to play when the convoy module hast something
to announce.

Defaults to nothing
menuName Name of the menu that the Convoy module installs for players if the
module’s GUI option is enabled.

Defaults to “Convoys”
hasGUI When set to false, Convoy’s interactive menu is disabled

Defaults to true (interactive menus are enabled)
listOwn When reporting convoys, include friendly (own coalition) convoys.
When set to true will also report a newly starting, or the arrival at
destination of a convoy for the own side

Defaults to true (friendly convoys are included)
listEnemy When reporting convoys, include enemy convoys.
When set to true will also report a newly starting, or the arrival at
destination of a convoy from enemy side

Defaults to true (enemy convoys are included)
listNeutral When reporting convoys, include neutral convoys.
Defaults to true (neutral convoys are included)

attachTo: Name of the radioMainMenu trigger zone to attach the convoy menu to.
Only applicable if hasGUI is enabled. Requires that the radioMenu
module has loaded.


Defaults to <none>
redConvoy# (Direct) output that is set to the number of red convoys that are
currently alive.

Defaults to <none>
blueConvoy# (Direct) output that is set to the number of blue convoys that are
currently alive

Defaults to <none>
neutralConvoy# (Direct) output that is set to the number of neutral convoys that are
currently alive

Defaults to <none>
allConvoy# (Direct) output that is set to the total number of convoys (red + blue +
neutral) that are currently alive

```
Defaults to <none>
```
_5.3.47.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
convoy** Marks this zone as a convoy Zone.
All Ground groups that have at least one vehicle inside this zone
become convoys. You define the route and the orders for those
groups using Mission Editor. If more than one ground vehicle group
is inside the convoy zone, one is chosen by random each time the
zone is signaled to spawn.
All helicopter groups inside this zone become possible helicopter
escorts. Only one group (or less, see _pEscort_ ) is chosen to escort
the convoy in its way. Remember to set up the helicopter’s loadout.

**MANDATORY**
coalition The coalition that the convoys spawned by this zone belong to.
Defaults to NEUTRAL
dynamic EXPERIMENTAL – do not use
Identical
unique

```
EXPERIMENTAL – do not use
```
preWipe When set to true, all previously spawned convoys are removed from
the mission.
Defaults to false (spawns do not remove previous convoy)
endWipe When set to true, a convoy that reaches their last waypoint is
removed from the map. This will prevent vehicles massing around
the last waypoint when multiple instances of the same convoy reach
their destination.
Defaults to true (convoys are removed when they reach the last
waypoint)
killWipeDelay Delay (in seconds) for escorting helicopters to be removed after all
ground vehicles of the convoy are destroyed. This allows you to
have the escort helicopter loiter like ‘angry hornets’ for a while
before they, too, are removed.
Defaults to 300 (5 Minutes)
pEscort Probability (in Percent) that the convoy is escorted by helicopters.
This requires that at least one helicopter group is present inside the
convoy zone. For example, if pEscort to 25, there is a 25% chance


**Name Description**
that a convoy when spawned also receives an escort.
If pEscort is smaller than 1, that convoy never receives a helicopter
escort.
Defaults to 100 (always escort if helicopters are present)
onStart If set to true, a convoy is spawned from this zone at the start of the
mission.
Defaults to false (no spawn on start)
wpUpdates If set to true, coalitions receive an update each time that a convoy
reaches a waypoint. There are separate messages for the first
waypoint (when the convoy is spawned), last waypoint (at least one
ground unit of the convoy reaches destination), and in-between
waypoints.
When a convoy reaches the penultimate waypoint (and assuming
that there are at least 3 waypoints in the convoy’s route), the enemy
coalition also receives a separate notification.
Defaults to true (waypoint updates are sent)
attackWarnings If set to true, the module sends out warnings to the coalition that
owns the convoy when it gets attacked and at least one unit is
destroyed. Only one warning per attacked convoy is sent within 5
minutes.
Defaults to true (coalition receives warnings)
spawn? Input. When triggered, this zone spawns a new convoy
Defaults to <none>
dead! Output: flag to change when all ground vehicles of a convoy
spawned by this zone are destroyed.
Defaults to <none>
attacked! Output: flag to change when ground vehicles of a convoy have been
destroyed since the last check, and the convoy isn’t completely
destroyed.
Defaults to <none>
arrived! Output: flag to change when the convoy (i.e. at least one living
ground unit) has reached its destination.
Defaults to <none>
convoyMethod DML method that is sent to the output.
Defaults to “inc”
convoyTriggerMethod DML method that triggers the input.
Defaults to “change”

_5.3.47.5 Using the module_
To use this module in your mission, add its script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME
- Add a ‘ _convoy’_ attribute
- add other attributes as required.
- Add at least one group of vehicles into the trigger zone, and define its route
- Optional: add helicopter groups that are to function as escorts and set up their
    loadout and fuel.


### 5.3.48 WHpersistence

_5.3.48.1 Description_
This module requires the “persistence” module.

Like the module unitPersistence, this module transparently saves and restores the current
status (inventory) of all airfield warehouses in a mission. If you don’t work with warehouse
inventories, you also don’t need this module.

**Note:**
A glaring oversight in DCS’s Warehouse API (which appears amateurish to begin with)
disallows saving (or accessing) warehouses that are not attached directly to a warehouse.
So if you went to the trouble of setting up **static warehouses** that supply an airfield, those
warehouses **can not be saved**. It’s incomprehensible how ED managed to completely
fumble something this fundamental.

This module currently has no interface for mission creators, it has an all-or-nothing
approach: add it to the mission, and the mission persists or loads the inventory of all
warehouses every time that persistence is invoked. Don’t add it to a mission, and inventories
are ignored by persistence.

_5.3.48.2 Dependencies_
Requires dcsCommon, cfxZones, and persistence.

_5.3.48.3 Persistence_
This module persist/loads all inventories of all warehouses.

_5.3.48.4 Module Configuration_
None.

_5.3.48.5 ME Attributes_
None.

_5.3.48.6 Using the module_
Add WHpersistence to your mission via a DOSCRIPT action at MISSION START


### 5.3.49 Usher

_5.3.49.1 Description_
The “usher” module solely exists to create signals as a response to player events; it’s a
‘yeller module that you can use to have your mission react appropriately to such events,
which is especially helpful in server-based multiplayer missions that can run for a long time.
Usher generates “first-time” and “normal” player events around players joining and leaving a
mission/coalition/unit. “One-time” player events are events such as a specific player entering
the mission for the first time, a player entering a specific module type for the first time, or
similar. Usher can detect the following events happening, and send out commands, display
messages and even (in certain cases) play sound files.

Normal player events are players joining and leaving a coalition during the course of a
mission. Additionally, Usher provides direct outputs for the number of players per coalition
for those missions that can use this information.

##### BASIC ABILITIES

Usher can detect the following first-time events and send out signals/commands

- A **specific player connects** to the mission for the first time. In this case, usher
    reacts to the player’s name (e.g. “CFRAG”). If no player of this name has entered the
    mission before, Usher reacts.
- A player is the **first to** connect to (i.e. take a seat in an aircraft) a **coalition**. This
    means that there are three possible events: one each for Red, Blue and Neutral.
- A player is the first to occupy a specific unit (by unit name, e.g. “Aerial- 1 - 1”)
- A player is the first to occupy a unit in a specific group (by name, e.g. “Snails”)
- A player is the first to occupy a specific aircraft type (e.g. “A-10A”)

Additionally, usher provides direct outputs for

- The number of players in the red faction
- The number of players in the blue faction
- The number of players in the neutral faction
- The total number of players in the mission


Finally, Usher can provide commands/signals for the following player events:

##### DYNAMIC MESSAGING ABILITIES

Several player events directly support outputting text messages to the player/group/coalition
who triggered the event. Similar to _messenger_ and _valet_ , these messages also support
wildcard dynamic text as follows:

- <n> - new line (“carriage return” for oldschoolers)
- <p> - player name, e.g. “CFRAG”
- <t> - type of unit, e.g. “A-10A” (warning: in messenger, this is used to display current
    time, which is not available to usher)
- <g> - name of the group that player is member of, e.g. “Boomers”
- <C> - name of players coalition in UPPER CASE, e.g. “RED”
- <c> - name of players coalition in lower case, e.g. “red”
- <E> name of enemy of player’s coalition in UPPPER CASE, e.g. “BLUE”
- <e> name of enemy of player’s coalition in lower case, e.g. “blue”
-

##### OTHER ABILITIES

- Usher can provide highly special, dynamic ‘commands’ based on the name of a
    player, a unit, or unit type. This is for advanced mission uses.
- Usher fully supports persistence, so should you enable persistence for a mission,
    Usher’s records are kept across multiple mission starts, and it will remember
    completed first-time events.
- Usher supports most wildcards for dynamic text messages that is can display for
    some of its events
- Usher can provide signals/commands when players leave or join the mission (more
    precisely: if the total number of players in the mission increases or goes down).

##### WARNING:

Due to the way that DCS functions, the results of usher can be dramatically different in
single-player versus (server-based) multiplayer. This is because in single-player, “time
stops” when a player changes slots and/or coalition. In single-player, different player events
can coalesce into a single event. Let us look at the sequence of events in multiplayer first:

Let’s say that a player occupies blue A-10 unit “Angel-1” and then re-slots to blue Hornet
“Snail-1”. In multiplayer, this generates the following events

- Player clicks on ‘SELECT ROLE’
- Player clicks on any other plane slot than the one that they currently occupy
- (immediately) Player leaves the mission,
- the number of players (total) and number of players (blue) each drop by one,
    commands are sent to pNum# and blueNum#
- Player chooses F/A- 18
- Player clicks on BRIEFING, then FLY
- (immediately) Player enters mission in F/A- 18


- The number of players (total) and the number of players (blue) each increase by one,
    commands are sent to pNum# and blueNum#

So, in a server-hosted mission, your mission sends out at least four commands during that
time.

Let’s contrast this with a single-player mission:

- Player clicks on ‘SELECT ROLE’ – **time is frozen** , no processing occurs
- Player chooses F/A- 18
- Player clicks on BRIEFING, then FLY
- (immediately) Player enters mission in F/A- 18 **– time unfreezes,** processing
    commences
- Upon checking, usher sees that the number of players hasn’t changed, and there is
    the same number of players for blue as before.

In single-player, changing the slot to a same-coalition plane creates fewer (even none)
signals/commands compared to multiplayer. If you create multiplayer missions you should at
least be aware of this difference in behavior.

**Note**
The name of this module refers to the occupation. My reference as a ‘yeller module refers to
DML’s “yeller model” and should not be understood as a reference nor ‘clever’ word play on
any similarly-named artists, of whose existence I was made aware of.

_5.3.49.2 Dependencies_
Usher requires dcsCommon and cfxZones

_5.3.49.3 Module Configuration_
Usher is entirely configuration driven. To configure the module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “usherConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.
Defaults to false
coaMsg Message to display to the coalition that the player just joined, player
has to be first player who joined that coalition.
Defaults to “Welcome, <p>, your <t> is the first to join
<c>.”
coaEvent If set to true, the first player (but not subsequent) who joins a particular
side (red, blue, neutral) triggers an event
Defaults to false (no coalition first join event)
coaRed! Triggered if coaEvent is true and the first player joins RED
Defaults to “none”
coaBlue! Triggered if coaEvent is true and the first player joins BLUE


Defaults to “none”
coaNeutral! Triggered if coaEvent is true and the first player joins NEUTRAL
Defaults to “none”
playerEvent If set to true, the first time that a player (identified by name) joins the
mission an event is triggered

```
SPECIAL EVENT
A special signal/command that is equal to the player’s name,
prepended with a prefix is sent. For example, if the player’s name is
CFRAG, and the prefix is “u:”, a command “u:CFRAG” is sent (the value
of the flag named u:CFRAG is increased by one)
```
Defaults to true (every player’s first join generates an event)
playerMsg Message to display the first time that a named player joins the mission
and playerEvent is set to true.
Defaults to “Welcome <p>!”
playerSound Name of the sound file to play to the unit when a named player first
joins the mission and playerEvent is set to true
Defaults to <none>
Player! Output to send a signal/command to when a named player joins for the
first time and playerEvent is set to true
Defaults to <none>
unitEvent If set to true, the first time that a player occupies a unit (identified by
unit name), an event is created
Defaults to false (no first time unit occupation events)
unitMsg Message to display for the unit when it is occupied by a player for the
first time.

```
SPECIAL EVENT
A special signal/command that is equal to the unit’s name, prepended
with a prefix is sent. For example, if the unit’s name is “Aero- 1 - 1” and
the prefix is “u:”, a command “u:Aero- 1 - 1” is sent (the value of the flag
named u:Aero- 1 - 1 is increased by one)
```
Defaults to "Welcome <p>, you are <u>, part of <c> <g> (a
flight of <t>)."
groupEvent If set to true, the first time that a player occupies a unit in a group
(identified by group name), an event is created.
This is different from unitEvent in that only the first unit in the unit’s
group that is occupied by a player for the first time generates an event.
Defaults to false (no first time unit in a group occupation events)
groupMsg Message to display for the unit when it is occupied by a player for the
first time.

```
SPECIAL EVENT
A special signal/command that is equal to the group’s name, prepended
with a prefix is sent. For example, if the group’s name is “Pounders”
and the prefix is “u:”, a command “u:Pounders” is sent (the value of the
flag named u:Pounders is increased by one)
```
Defaults to "Welcome <p>, you are <u>, part of <c> <g> (a
flight of <t>)." (same as unitMsg)
typeEvent If set to true, the first time that a player occupies an aircraft of a
particular type (e.g. A-10A), an event is created.


Defaults to false (no events for first-time type occupation)
typeMsg Message to display to this unit when its type is occupied by a player for
the first time.

```
SPECIAL EVENT
A special signal/command that is equal to the unit’s type, prepended
with a prefix is sent. For example, if the unit’s type is “A-10A” and the
prefix is “u:”, a command “u:A-10A” is sent (the value of the flag named
u:A-10A is increased by one)
```
Defaults to "Welcome to your <t>, <p>!"
Prefix The prefix to apply to all special event commands.
Defaults to “u:” (for ‘usher:’)
red# Direct output to send the number of players on the red side
Defaults to <none>
blue# Direct output to send the number of players on the blue side
Defaults to <none>
neutral Direct output to send the number of players on the neutral side
Defaults to <none>
pNum# Direct output to send the total number of players
Defaults to <none>
join! Output to send a signal/command when the total number of players
increases
Defaults to <none>
leave! Output to send a signal/command when the total number of players
decreases
Defaults to <none>
ups Number of updates per second.
Defaults to 0.1 (every 10 seconds)
method DML method to apply to outputs
Default to “inc”

_5.3.49.4 ME Attributes_
None

_5.3.49.5 Using the module_
Add the usher module to your mission. Add a usherConfig zone, and change attributes
according to your requirements


### 5.3.50 Fogger

_5.3.50.1 Description_
This module allows you to easily control ground fog

Fogger supports heavy randomization (if you want that) and can set a mission’s visibility
from perfect to terrible at the drop of a hat. Mission designers set up the fog conditions
(including randomization) and simply send a command/signal to a fog trigger zone to start
the fogging (DCS supports a transition period from current conditions to new, and of course
so does DML).

_5.3.50.2 Dependencies_
Fogger requires dcsCommon and cfxZones

_5.3.50.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the Fogger module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “foggerConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.
Defaults to false
ups Number of times per second that fogger checks to see if a command
was sent to one of it’s zones.
Defaults to 1 (one)

_5.3.50.4 ME Attributes_

**Name Description
fog?** This input triggers transition to the current visibility to the fog
settings defined below.

**MANDATORY**
triggerMethod DML Method for the input


**Name Description**

Defaults to “change”
visibility Number of meters for visibility. The lower the number, the worse
visibility. If set to a value below 100, this turns off fog-
Value can be a range (e.g. 1000-2000). When specified as a range,
a random value between the two values is generated each time that
fog? is triggered

Defaults to 0 (no fog)
thickness Thickness of the fog in meters, as defined by DCS. It is currently
insufficiently specified by ED if this means MSL or AGL. Since fog is
a global map setting, it seems prudent to assume that the value for
thickness is meters above medial sea level.
When this value is less than 100, fig is turned off
Value can be a range (e.g. 200-600). When specified as a range, a
random value between the two values is generated each time that
fog? is triggered

Defaults to 0 (no fog)
lcl When set to true, the altitude at the zone’s center is added to the
fog’s thickness, allowing you to create ‘local’ thickness. If, for
example, you set thickness to 100, and the zone is positioned at
220 MSL, the fog’s thickness is set to 220 + 100 = 320.
This is useful for maps that have many airfields in higher regions
(e.g. NTTR)

```
Note: currently in DCS, fog is a global setting, and there is no true
local fog. The setting from a fog zone applies to the entire map.
```
Defaults to false (non-local)
duration Time in seconds that it takes from the moment that the new fog
settings are triggered until the fog is fully established. DCS will
transition to the new settings dynamically over time, so you can use
this feature to have fog ‘creep in’ over time
Like thickness and visibility, duration supports a range to randomize
the duration.

```
Defaults to 1 second
```
_5.3.50.5 Using the module_
To use this module in your mission, add its script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

To use

- Add a trigger zone in ME
- Add a ‘ _fog?’_ attribute and supply a command name
- add other attributes as required.


### 5.3.51 smoking

_5.3.51.1 Description_
The smoking module allows _player_ aircraft access to ‘smoke trail’ so that they can ‘write in
the sky’, train complex maneuvers, and increase their visibility during training.

The module allows players to turn their aircraft’s smoke trail on and off at will. The color of
the smoke trail is determined by the mission designer.

**NOTE:**
The aircraft do _not_ have to be equipped with a smoke generator (“smokewinders” et al) to
gain smoke trail abilities. DML’s smoking module does not interfere nor override such a
capability should it be present in a plane or helicopter.

##### NOTE:

The smoking zones do not imbue AI aircraft with a smoke trail ability.

##### NOTE

Due to DCS constraints, smoke trails require **single-unit player aircraft groups**. The
behavior of this module may become erratic if you add more than one aircraft to a player
group.


##### BASIC FUNCTIONALITY

When the module is included into to mission, **all** player aircraft gain the ability to turn the
smoke trail on and off via the communications menu.

The color of all smoke trails is governed by the module’s config zone.

##### CURTAILING SMOKE TRAILS

If there are **no** trigger zones with the “smoking” key attribute present in the mission, all
player aircraft gain the ‘smoke trail’ ability that can be controlled via communications.

As soon as there is at least one such trigger zone, only aircraft that spawn within such a
zone receive the smoke trail ability, and the smoke behaves as detailed by the zone’s
attributes.

##### TURNING OFF THE SMOKE TRAIL GUI

The config zone allows you to entirely disable the modules communication interface. Since
there currently is no way to trigger a player plane’s ‘DML smoking’ trail abilities, this option is
reserved for future expansions of the smoking module (which, for example may force trails
on for player aircraft that enter a trail-triggering zone, another possible future enhancement)

##### THE CURIOUSLY DISAPPEARING TRAIL (KNOWN BUG)

DML’s smoking module bases its function on DCS API’s _ctfColorTag()_ function, which is
woefully under-documented and **highly erratic**. At this time (early 2025), smoke turns off
should you descend below the altitude that you turned smoke on. This bug was reported in
May 2023 to ED.

_5.3.51.2 Dependencies_
smoking requires dcsCommon and cfxZones.

Should you want to take advantage of the ‘AttachTo:” ability, you must also include
radioMenus (and its dependent modules) into your mission

_5.3.51.3 Module Configuration_
To configure the smoking module via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone on the map in ME
- Name it “smokingConfig” (note: name must match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose Show debugging information.
Defaults to false
color Color of the smoke trail that follows an aircraft that has engaged it.
Valid colors are (without quotes “”)

- “green” or “ 0 ”


- “red” or “ 1 ”
- “white” or “ 2 ”
- “orange” or “ 3 ”
- “blue” or “ 4 ”
- “rnd” or “random” or “?”

Defaults to “white”
alt For later expansion, currently not expected to function

menuName Name of the menu that is installed into player’s communications→Other
(or deeper when using “attachTo:”) that contains the commands to turn
smoke trials on an off

Defaults to “Smoke Trail”
GUI When set to false, no player receives access to the communications
menu that controls smoke trails

Defaults to true (players have access to the smoke trail menu)
attachTo: Name of the radioMainMenu trigger zone to attach the smoking menu
to. Only applicable if GUI is enabled. Requires that the radioMenu
module has loaded.

```
Defaults to <none>
```
_5.3.51.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
smoking** Marks this zone as a smoke trail designator that controls the
behavior of all player aircraft that spawn inside this zone.

```
Note that the presence of a single zone with this attribute
switches all aircraft in the mission to require individual
‘smoking’ designations. A player aircraft that does not spawn
inside such a zone no longer has access to the smoke trail function
```
```
The value of this attribute defines the color of the smoke trail.
```
```
Valid colors are (without quotes “”)
```
- “green” or “ 0 ”
- “red” or “ 1 ”
- “white” or “ 2 ”
- “orange” or “ 3 ”
- “blue” or “ 4 ”
- “rnd” or “random” or “?”

```
Defaults to “white”
```
**MANDATORY**
alt For later expansion, currently not expected to function


_5.3.51.5 Using the module_
To use this module in your mission, add its script to the mission as a DOSCRIPT action
during Mission Start.

All player aircraft now can activate a smoke trail via communications.

To limit access to smoke trails to some player aircraft

- Add a trigger zone in ME around the player aircraft(s) that should be able to use the
    smoke trail ability
- Add a ‘ _smoking’_ attribute and supply a color
- Add more trigger zones with a smoking attribute around other planes as required.


## 5.4 “Big Ticket” (“drop-in”) Modules

DML stand-alone features are drop-in modules that provide - out-of-the-box - specific
capabilities without requiring additional set-up (although mission designers can curtail these
modules to their requirements with configuration- and data zones).

### 5.4.1 Player Score / PlayerScoreUI

_5.4.1.1 Description_
Player Score is a drop-in module for score keeping with a host of features:

- Score is kept separately for players and factions. Players can change their faction
    during missions without upsetting their (or the faction’s) score
