unusual locations like gas platforms, mountain tops, overgrown or steep inclines etc.
Sometimes, using these locations can pay off greatly. Usually, they don’t. Sometimes,
adding a single helipad can help. Sometimes, things simply wont work out with current DCS
AI:

While it’s no problem to have a civHelo flight originate from an offshore gas or oil platform,
DCS’s AI can’t reliably land a helicopter there. Spawning or landing on steep inclines is
going to end in disaster more often than not, etc. Make sure that you test your departure and
arrival locations before you publish the mission. That being said, a crashed civHelo
helicopter does create any problems for the module, it merely proceeds to create a new flight
at the next convenient point in time.

**Maximum Traffic**
At any time, two factors control the amount of civHelo flights that are in the air at the same
time:

- The number of free departure and arrival zones. Each flight requires one free
    departure zone, and one free arrival zone that is within maxDist and at least minDist
    away. After take-off, a flight relinquishes the departure zone, but the arrival zone
    remains locked until the flight lands there or is destroyed. So if you are wondering
    why there are so few flights in the air, check the number of civHelo zones on the
    map.


- The global maxFlight puts a hard cap on the number of flights that can be created,
    provided enough free civHelo zones are available. This is to allow mission designers
    to limit the amount of performance that may be allocated to civilian helicopter traffic
    AI (which is not a great amount, but being able to limit the impact seems like a good
    idea)

_5.4.23.2 Dependencies_
civHelo requires dcsCommon and cfxZones

_5.4.23.3 Module Configuration_
To configure aspects of scribe via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “civHeloConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages
Default is “false”
country The country ID to whom all helicopters belong.
Defaults to 82 (Combined UN peace force)
ups Updates per second.
Defaults to 1/10 (1 Update every 10 seconds)
maxFlights Maximum number of helicopters simultaneously under the control
civHelo.
Defaults to 5
landingDuration Time between the point when a civHelo-controlled helicopter initiates a
landing, and when it is removed from the map.
Defaults to 180 (seconds = 3 minutes)
alt Altitude in meters (AGL) that flights use to fly between departure and
arrival points.
Defaults to 100 (300 ft)
speed Speed (in m/s) that flights use to fly between departure and arrival
points.
Defaults to 30 (m/s = 108 km/h = 60 knots)
maxDist When picking destination zones for a flight, that destination must not be
further away than this distance (in meters)
Defaults to 60000 m = 60 km = 37 miles
minDist When picking destination zones for a flight, that destination must not be
closer than this distance (in meters)
Defaults to 1000 m = 1 km = 0.6 miles
types DCS types that civHelo can choose from for its flights. Types are
separated by comma (“,”)
Defaults to
CH-47D, CH-53E, Ka-27, Mi-24V, Mi-26, Mi-28N, Mi-8MT, OH-58D,
SA342L, SH-60B, UH-1H, UH-60A


_5.4.23.4 ME Attributes_
You place civHelo zones on the map to define where helo flights can originate from and
where they fly to. Note that with civHeloConfig you can control how far apart these zones
have to be at a minimum, and how far they may be apart at maximum, so you can build
multiple ‘civHelo hubs’ on your map.

**Name Description
civHelo** Tells DML that this zone is a civHelo zone that can be used for flights

**Mandatory**
land If set to true, civHelo flights can land here (use the zone as a
destination for a flight)

Defaults to TRUE (civHelo flight can use this as destination)
start If set to true, civHelo flights can take off from here (use the zone as a
starting point for a flight)

Defaults to TRUE (civHelo flights can use this a departure point for a
flight)
hot If set to true, civHelo flights that depart from here start hot. Otherwise,
the flight is cold-started

Defaults to FALSE (flight is cold-started)
types The DCS types of helicopters, separated by comma (“,”) that can start
from here.

```
Example: Mi-8MT, SA342L, UH-1H
```
```
Defaults to the same types that are supplied with the config zone.
```
_5.4.23.5 Using the module_
Copy the civHelo source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail civHelo’s behavior.

Add at least two trigger zones with a “civHelo” attribute that can serve as source and
destination for flights. Make sure that those two zones fulfil both minDist and maxDist
constraints.


### 5.4.24 Slotty

_5.4.24.1 Introduction_
The excellent “Simple Slot Block” script developed by Ciribob has allowed mission designers
since 2017 to create missions with player slots that can be blocked. This allows, for
example, missions with airfields that can be captured and player aircraft on these airfields to
only become available to players when their side has captured the airfield/FARP.

DML’s _ssbClient_ module implements the “SSB protocol” to allow mission designers just that:
aircraft slots being tied to conquerable airfields/FARPs. Other modules in DML use the same
“SSB Protocol” to deny player access to aircraft after they crash, and there are many other
fun uses for using SSB in your missions to control player’s slot control. Until recently,
though, this meant two things:

- The server that runs the mission must have Ciribob’s “SSB” server script installed.
    Installation is simple, and third-party hosting providers install SSB by default because
    it is so popular.
- The mission itself must be run in multi-player for the server SSB script to take effect.
    In single-player SSB does not work for the simple reason that the server isn’t active.

The ‘slotty’ module makes above points moot. It implements the SSB protocol for single-
player and multiplayer on the client side, and allows slot-blocking for single and multiplayer.
Even better, it chips in in there is no SSB installed on the server and provides a fallback
mechanism so you can now create missions that rely on SSB without having to bother if SSB
was installed.

_5.4.24.2 Description_
Slotty provides **single-player slot blocking** and a fallback for **multiplayer slot-blockin** g if
the server that is running your mission does not have SSB installed.

Slotty conforms to the **SSB (“Simply slot block”) protocol** and kicks players that are trying
to ‘slot’ into an aircraft that is marked as not available. Modules like _ssbClient_ , for example,
use this protocol to prevent players from slotting into aircraft that are located on airfields or
FARPs that belong to the enemy.

If the server running your mission hasn’t SSB activated/installed, or the mission is run as
single-player, the SSB module can’t prevent players from slotting into those aircraft. This is
where slotty comes in. If you add the module to your mission, that mission automatically
gains smart slot blocking for single player, and a fallback for multi-player should SSB not be
installed. Note that the player experience is better if SSB is running for multiplayer. In all
other instances, slotty will jump in and pick up the slack.

Slotty is a drop-in-and-forget module that does not require any configuration.

_5.4.24.3 Dependencies_
None. Slotty doesn’t even require DML


_5.4.24.4 Module Configuration_
None. Slotty works out of the box

_5.4.24.5 ME Attributes_
None.

_5.4.24.6 Interactive enable/disable_
You can disable slotty from blocking slots by setting the value of the flag “ **noSlotty** ” to any
value greater than 0 (Zero)

_5.4.24.7 Using the module_
Add Slotty to your mission during AT START as a DOSCRIPT. Smile.


### 5.4.25 Reaper

_5.4.25.1 Description_
The reaper module allows you to quickly place a drone in the air over a location and have it
automatically lase enemy ground units. All that it requires is placing a trigger zone and add
an attribute.

That being said, reaper is a fully fledged DML module and comes with all the bells and
whistles that you’d expect, including a UI.

**Reaper or Predator**
There currently are two different drone types that you can choose from. By default, the
reaper module uses an MQ-9 Reaper. Alternatively, you can choose an RQ-1A Predator
type drone. Which type of drone you choose has no impact on lasing. A Reaper has a higher
service ceiling and moves a bit faster than a Predator drone. Neither is equipped for combat;
both merely provide lasing support (and battlefield intelligence if the map settings are set to
‘fog of war’).

**Operational area**
With placing the trigger zone on
the map, you also define the
area of operation and flight path
of the drone. When you place a
circular trigger zone, the drone
will fly a racetrack pattern
between the two points that are
defined by the ‘bottom left’
(southern-western) and ‘upper
right’ (northern-eastern) points
that form the corners of the
square that completely
encompasses the trigger zone
circle. This means that,
statistically, the drone will spend a large fraction of the time over the center of the target
zone. Due to the nature of drones and flight, the drones will extend their flight path slightly
over the two points; if you choose the target zone’s size to be too small, the drone will
extend to the minimal size possible to maintain a good pattern.


Similarly, if you define the area of
operations as a quad-based trigger
zone, the drone will create a racetrack
pattern from the ‘bottom left’ corner of
the smallest surrounding rectangle to
the ‘top right’ corner, again making the
drone always cross over the center of
the zone.

Note that once a drone has detected a
target and starts tracking (lasing) it, it will alter its flight path to continue to track the target.

Note also that drones are sitting ducks when it comes to enemy fire, be it SAM or aircraft,
they do not defend themselves (nor do they have the means to).

**Auto-Lasing**
Drones automatically scan for enemy units, and once they find a target, will automatically
lase the target. Unless specified otherwise, the laser code is 1688. Once a target is found,
the drone continues to lase the target until it is destroyed.

**Target Cycling**
Once a drone engages a group, players can tell the drone to cycle the target. Cycling will
cause the drone to choose the next target in the group (or first target in the group after the
last). Note that all cycled targets are within the same group, and the drone will only look for
other groups once all targets have been destroyed, or have otherwise disappeared (e.g.
hidden from view).

**Visual Range**
A drone’s visual range is determined by its altitude. As a rule of thumb, the range is equal to
its altitude: if a drone is at 5000m (15’000 ft) altitude, it can detect targets up to 5000m
(15’000 feet away). Note that terrain can mask a unit’s visibility. This means that even
though a unit may be within detection range, a drone still may fail to detect it if it’s masked by
terrain.

As soon as a drone detects and locks on to a target, it sends a message to all players from
the same side with information about the target’s location and laser code:

**Reaper’s built-in “Drone Command” UI (Individual Drones)**
Reaper comes with a built-in UI (that you can disable or move to other locations) to manage
all drones. Using this UI you can give players access to all drones that reaper manages


Players select the drone (zone) that interests them, and then receive a drone-individual
command menu:

Note that since DCS is limited to 10 menu items per menu, Reaper’s built-in UI supports at
most 10 Reaper zones per side. If you want to use more, you’ll need to build your own
reaper control menus via the radioMenu module (see the ‘secret drone’ option in the demo)

Per drone, you have three options:

- _Launch the drone_
    If the drone is already launched, nothing happens. If cost is activated and the bank
    module has loaded, funds are checked and the drone is only launched if there are
    sufficient funds available. After launch, the funds are withdrawn from that side.
- _Status Update_
    The drone sends a report about the target it is currently lasing, and the entire group’s
    composition:
- _Cycle Target_
    This causes the drone to step through all targets in the group. Players can use this to
    prioritize targets in the group. In above example, cycling will cause the drone to
    target the T-90 next, then the BTR-80, then back to the AAV7. The drone cannot
    cycle to other groups.

```
NOTE:
Some DCS aircraft (for example the Kiowa) simulate a video feed from the drone.
Since this is only simulated, and there currently is no method to determine which unit
a drone targets, cycling the target will not actually change targets in the video feed,
not will the displayed unit be guaranteed to be the target that the drone is currently
targeting. When DCS provides an API to interface with drones, this function may be
updated.
```
Reaper’s UI supports radioMenu’s radioMainMenu layer via the attachTo: attribute, so you
can move the reaper menu from the “Other” level to others that better suit your mission.


**Drone Summary (Status)**
Reaper can provide a summary status of all drones, split into three groups:

- Tracking/lasing (with target type and location of target, plus laser code)
- Loitering (with drone name and type)
- Drones that are ready to launch (with required funds if that is enabled)

Note that all drones from reaper zones that have their statusUI attribute set to false will not
show up in this report. Here’s an example:

**DML triggering**
All reaper zones provide inputs to selectively trigger a drone launch, drone status message,
or target cycling.

Additionally, reaper also supports triggering a fully drone status report (all drones) per
coalition.

**Integration with other modules**
Reaper provides automatic integration with the following modules:

- **radioMenu**
    Reaper supports an “attachTo:” attribute to relocate reaper’s “Drone Command”
    menu to a radioMainMenu zone of your choice
- **bank**
    When reaper’s cost ability is enabled and the bank module is present, reaper only
    launches a drone when the there is enough funds in the bank for the faction, and
    automatically deducts the amount.

_5.4.25.2 Dependencies_
reaper requires dcsCommon and cfxZones

_Optionally_ requires radioMenus (for attachtTo:) or bank (for priced launches)

_5.4.25.3 Module Configuration_
To configure reaper via a configuration zone,


- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “reaperConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

Default is “false”
actionSound Name of the sound file to play when a communication is made (e.g. a
drone is launched or starts tracking a unit)

Defaults to <none>
UI When set to false, reaper does not install its own UI for getting drone
status reports or launching drones.

Defaults to true (reaper installs its own UI)
menuName Name of the reaper menu (when active)

Defaults to “Drone Command”
useCost Use reaper’s drone cost ability. Requires that the bank module is also
present in the miz. When active, a faction can only launch drones if they
have sufficient funds. Drone cost is set individually per reaper zone.

Defaults to
blueStatus? Input to trigger a status report of all drones for the BLUE side (only
visible to BLUE players). Lists only those drones that have not set their
statusUI attribute to false.

Defaults to <none>
redStatus? Input to trigger a status report of all drones for the RED side (only
visible to RED players). Lists only those drones that have not set their
statusUI attribute to false.

Defaults to <none>
attachTo: If present, the reaper UI attaches to the trigger zone that is named as
value. That trigger zone must have a _radioMainMenu_ defined, and
radioMenu must run before reaper.

_5.4.25.4 ME Attributes_
You place reaper zones on the map to define where the drone is going to patrol. The patrol
route is defined as the ‘cross section’ of the zone (meaning the size of the zone is relevant).
The reaper zone defines all drone-individual properties.

**Name Description
reaper** Tells DML that this zone is a reaper zone that is patrolled by a drone.
The value of this attribute defines the type of drone. Possible values are

- Reaper – the drone is a type “MQ-9 Reaper”, max altitude
    9500m (28000ft), speed 200 km/h (110kts)
- Predator – the drone is a type “RQ-1A Predator”, max altitude
    7500m (22000ft), speed 120 km/h (65 kts)


**Name Description**

```
Defaults to “Reaper”
```
**Mandatory**
alt Altitude (in meters) for the drone’s racetrack pattern. Note that the
altitude also defines the drone’s visual range, which is roughly equal to
the altitude. Predator and Reapers have different ceiling altitudes.
coalition Faction that owns the drone. Possible values are

- 1 or “red”
- 2 or “blue”
- Note that neutral drones are automatically switched to BLUE

Defaults to BLUE
onStart If set to true, the drone is launched on mission start, at no cost.

Defaults to true (drone launches at mission start)
code Laser code for lasing.

Defaults to 1688
doSmoke Visual aid for targeting. When a drone starts targeting a unit, it can also
place smoke at the location of the target. The smoke marker lasts circa
5 minutes and does not move with the unit.

Defaults to false (no smoke when marking units)
smokeColor Color of smoke to place at the location of the target. Valid values are

- 0 or “green”
- 1 or “red”
- 2 or “white”
- 3 of “orange”
- 4 or “blue”

Defaults to “blue”
cost Only used when config’s ‘useCost’ is enabled and the ‘bank’ module is
loaded. Cost in ‘fund units’ to launch a drone. Does not affect initial
launches if the onStart attribute is true

When actice, the cost is included in reaper’s launch UI
Defaults to 700
autoRespawn If a drone is disappeared (for any reason), it is instantly replaced with a
new one (for free) if this attribute is set to true

Defaults to false
launchUI If set to true, this reaper zone is included with the reaper UI

Defaults to true (zone is included with launch UI)
statusUI If set to true, this zone’s drone is included in the global status reports.

Defaults to true (drone is included in reaper status reports)
launch? Input that when triggered launches this zone’s drone. When cost
enabled and the bank module present, a funds check is performed and
the drone is only launched if sufficient funds are present.

```
Defaults to <none>
```

**Name Description**
status? Input that when triggered outputs this zone’s drone status to the owning
side.

Defaults to <none>
cycle? Input that when triggered changes the targeted unit in the group to the
next.
invisible When set to true, the drone is invisible to enemy AI so it won’t be
attacked by SAM nor enemy units.

_5.4.25.5 Using the module_
Copy the reaper source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail reaper’s behavior.

Add at least one trigger zone and add the ‘reaper’ attribute.


### 5.4.26 Inferno

_5.4.26.1 Description_
This module can create randomized, growing fires inside a zone that can be put out in
conjunction with the _airtank_ module. Inferno creates ‘fire cells’ inside a trigger zone that can
spread to other cells inside the zone (but not cross-zone), and can be used to create
impressive dynamic scenery (for example after an aircraft crashes in a forest) and provide
tasks for aerial firefighters.

Note that currently in DCS, all fires have _visual_ effects only: they do not affect (outside of
visibility) other units nor the local weather. There are no updrafts or air currents, the flames
(and smoke) are not affected by rotors, and likewise, flying through or inside a fire effect
does _not_ adversely affect units.

**INFERNO ABILITIES**

_Area of effect_
Mission authors use a trigger zone (circular or quad) to define the area in which the fire can
spread. It will never spread outside the zone, and the module provides logic to prevent fire
cells to sprout on rivers, lakes, roads, or deep water.

_Start & Spread_
Inferno models the conflagration inside the trigger zone as a collection of _fire cells_. How
many cells inhabit a zone is controlled by the _cellSize_ attribute in conjunction with the
surface area of the trigger zone. All infernos start from a single fire cell that starts small, and
then grows if there are potential fire cells located nearby.

To start the fire inside a zone, send a signal on the _ignite?_ input. The first
fire cell is either picked by random (if _rndLoc_ is true) or near the center of
the zone. Once a fire has grown sufficiently, it can spread to neighboring
cells, provided that the cell is still inside the trigger zone and eligible to
burn: it has

- has not been doused (via _airtank_ ),
- is not inside a body of water or on a road,
- has enough fuel and therefore has not burned out
- the number of burning cells in this zone is below the maximum


To model a fire cell, Inferno uses DCS’s ‘Fire FX’, a purely visual effect that is a 2D
‘billboard’: a virtual surface that always is oriented towards the viewer, with pixel art (flames
and smoke) drawn onto the billboard. For the biggest (DCS-provided) flame effects, these
billboards are some 100m wide, and therefore a fire cell defaults to side length of 100m.
Since fires in DCS are not volumetric, getting close to them is currently an underwhelming
experience. Hopefully, later DCS releases change that.

_Limiting spread of fire_
When you place a trigger zone and designate it as an inferno, the module defaults to a cell
size of 100m x 100m. Therefore, if your trigger zone is 2km x 3km in size, it can contain up
to 20x30 = 600 fire cells. If you place no restrictions on that zone, and set a match to the
zone (via ignite?), after a while it will be fully ablaze, with 600 flame and smoke effects.
Although possibly impressive, it can be quite taxing to any GPU. The _maxSpread_ attribute
caps the number of times that a fire can spread to provide more versatility and greater
random, inferno provides mission designers with the ability to restrict the number of fire cells
that a fire can spread to. This allows you to place an inferno over a large area, yet allow a
fire only to grow to a specific, smaller size for randomization and better performance. For
example, an inferno with a maxSpread of 7 can at most have 8 active fire cells inside: one
initial cell, plus 7 cells that it can spread to. If there are fewer cells, or the shape or ground
does not allow the fire to spread further, that inferno can’t reach its maximum size.

Fire cells spread from the center of one cell to the surrounding eight cells, using a
randomized propagation algorithm. A fire can only spread when enough criteria are met.
Note that currently, the direction of the prevailing winds are not considered.

_Zone Shape vs fire cells_
It is not always simple to correctly foresee how many fire cells fit into a trigger zone. All fire
cells are square, and for simplicity, DML first creates a rectangular axis-aligned bounding
box (AABB) around the trigger zone, and then partitions that rectangular area into squares of
_cellSize_. Only those squares whose center falls inside the trigger zone are regarded as fire
cells:

In the example above, only the green squares are considered fire cells, while the red
squares are discarded and will not be used to house a fire cell

_Iteration Speed_
Inferno simulates the spread (and dousing) of fires based on an internal clock called _fireTick_.
Each time it calculates the size of the fire for the next cycle. By default, each cycle lasts 10


seconds, so inferno calculates 6 cycles per minute. This allows fires to spread quickly (within
minutes) and respond quickly after being doused with water. If you plan to use inferno purely
for visual effects, I recommend that you increase iteration speed, as you do not need the
(small) overhead to calculate the next cycle.

If you plan to use inferno with the _airtank_ module to provide fire-fighting abilities, you may
want to think about tweaking the _fireTick_ value to adapt the response of fires to reality.
Setting it to 1 makes the fires spread and respond very fast, while setting it to larger values
like 20 makes the overall response more realistic but perhaps less fun.

_Fuel_
A fire cell in inferno consumes fuel while burning. When all fuel is consumed, that cell dies
out. This is used mainly in conjunction with the _airtank_ module, as the flame retardant that
the aircraft drop removes fuel from the fire cell. If you increase the amount of fuel per fire
cell, it takes more retardant per cell to douse the fire.

Fire zones can be set to _eternal_ – if enabled (default), a fire cell burns without consuming
fuel until an airtank delivers some retardant into the cell. From that moment on, the cell
consumes the remaining fuel. The time it takes for a fire cell to burn out is tied to the fireTick
attribute. A fire cell burns an amount of fuel proportional to the flame size per tick, so if your
tick time is longer, it burns longer with the same amount of fuel.

_Extinguished Cells / Extinguished Inferno / dousing_
Fire cells extinguish once they have burnt all fuel. Once a fire cell is extinguished, it can’t
reignite. Once all fire cells inside the inferno zone are extinguished, the zone can send a
signal via the _extinguished!_ output.
You can also douse an inferno by sending it a signal on the _douse?_ Input, which will
extinguish all fire cells that are currently burning.

Once the entire inferno has been extinguished, it can be re-ignited with the _ignite?_ Input. All
fire cells are reset and the fire is newly randomized if that option is enabled.

_5.4.26.2 Dependencies_
Inferno requires _dcsCommon_ and _cfxZones_.

_5.4.26.3 Module Configuration_
To configure inferno via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “infernoConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

```
Default is “false”
```

**Name Description**
fireTick Time (in seconds) per ‘burn cycle’. The larger the number, the longer a
fire burns.
Defaults to 10 (seconds)
cellSize Size (in meters) per side for the fire cells inside the zone. All fire cells
are square. This value can be overridden individually by inferno zones.

Defaults to 100 (meters, roughly the size of a maximum size fire effect
in DCS)
fire# Direct output that always contains the number of inferno zones that
have at least one burning fire cell inside them.
Defaults to <none>

_5.4.26.4 ME Attributes_
You can place inferno zones anywhere on the map except over deep water (well, you can,
but it won’t burn). The zone defines the area in which fire cells are placed, and the number of
cells inside the zone is determined by the zones size, shape and the _cellSize_ module config
attribute.

**Name Description
inferno** Tells DML that this zone is an inferno zone. The value of this attribute is
ignored.

**Mandatory**
cellSize Size (in meters) per side for the fire cells inside the zone. All fire cells
are square. This value overrides the value given in the config zone.

Defaults to the config value for cellSize.
fuel Amount of fuel for each fire cell inside the zone. Once a cell ignites, it
spends fuel while burning (except when _eternal_ is enabled, see below).
When all fuel is spent, the fire cell extinguishes and can’t re-ignite until
the entire inferno zone resets

Defaults to 100
rndLoc When set to true, the location of the first fire cell to ignite is randomized.
This can randomize shape and size of the resulting conflagration.

Defaults to true (randomized fire starting cell)
freeBorder When set to true, the fire cells inside the zone are surrounded by a
‘ring’ of ‘dud’ (unignitable). Use this to visually better align an inferno
with geologic peculiarities of the map.

Defaults to true (add an inside border)
eternal If set to true, fires inside a fire cell do not consume fuel until they
receive a signal from an outside source (usually the _airtank_ module),
making all cells, once ignited, eternal.

Defaults to true (eternal burn)
stagger When set to true, the center of the flame effect is randomized inside the
fire cell, making the entire inferno look more natural.

```
Defaults to true (randomized FX location)
```

**Name Description**
canSpread When set to true, a fire cell can ignite neighboring fire cells.

Defaults to true (fire can spread to other cells)
maxSpread The maximum amount of fire cells that a fire can spread to inside this
zone.

Defaults to infinite
onStart If set to true, the inferno in this zone is started when the mission starts
up

Defaults to false (inferno waits for ignite? input)
ignite? Input to tell this zone to start a fire by igniting a single fire cell inside. If
the zone has already started a fire, this signal is ignored.

Defaults to <none>
douse? Input to tell the zone to extinguish all actively burning fire cells inside
this zone, and reset it to re-start via _ignite?_

Defaults to <none>
extinguished! Output that bangs on the connected flag when all burning fire cells have
been extinguished and the inferno cell is no longer burning.

Defaults to <none>
impactSmoke TESTING ONLY
Places blue smoke markers on the location where the water hits the
ground

```
Defaults to false (turned OFF)
```
_5.4.26.5 Using the module_
Copy the inferno source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail inferno’s behavior.

Place at least one trigger zone and add the ‘inferno’ attribute.


### 5.4.27 Airtank

_5.4.27.1 Description_
Airtank is a drop-in module that facilitates firefighting by players using helicopters.

##### SUPPORTED UNITS

By default, airtank is configured to provide its fire-fighting abilities to the following DCS
modules with retardant capacities (in kg):

- UH- 1 H Huey (1200 kg)
- Mi-8 MT Hip (4000 kg)
- Mi-24P Hind (2400 kg)
- CH-47 Chinook (9000 kg)
- OH-58 Kiowa (500 kg)

Note that these capacities presume a 50% (i.e. _half_ - full) fuel tank; some helicopters will be
unable to lift off with full fuel tanks and a full retardant load.

You can change the supported types and capacities (e.g. to add the Blackhawk or remove a
supported type, or to change a type’s capacity) with the _airtanks_ attribute in the config zone.

##### HOW TO USE

The airtank module allows players to use certain
types of aircraft to be used for firefighting. For
this, it makes a number of functions available to
players:

- Loading flame retardant
- Dropping flame retardant
- Miscellaneous

_Loading flame retardant:_
There are two ways for players to load retardant/water:

- When inside a refill zone (an airtank zone): Via
    Communications→Other→Firefighting→Activate/Ready Intake to fill up your flame-
    retardant tanks. Filling your tanks is instantaneous. (Note that the “Firefighting” menu
    name, and location on the Other menu are customizable)


- When hovering over open water (lake/river), and at 10m AGL or less: Via
    Communications→Other→Firefighting→Activate/Ready Intake. Here, the pumps
    acquire 100 liters (kg) of water per second. For example, filling up the Huey’s
    retardant tank (1200l capacity) takes 12 seconds, while topping off the Hook at that
    rate takes 90 seconds. Pumps disengage automatically when the tank is full.

_Dropping flame retardant_
Plaers can drop the contents of their flame-retardant tank in multiple ways. Once started, the
process of unloading cannot be stopped, the tanks empty at a throughput of 1000l/second:

- **Manual drop**
    Via Communications→Other→Firefighting→Manual Release. This empties the
    contents of the helicopter’s tanks at 1000kg/sec until they are empty. The impact
    point is derived from the player’s current altitude, speed and direction.
- **Altitude-triggered**
    The player can only initiate this when above 300 m AGL (900 ft). To start the
    process, they have to choose Communications→Other→Firefighting→Arm Autodrop
    Trigger. Once the player’s altitude drops below 100m AGL (328ft), release is
    triggered.

**Note:**
Altitude-triggered release is more difficult, and intended for advanced pilots who can judge
their approach.

Once release has started, it ends after the _entire_ contents has been emptied. The drop
pumps are rated at 1000kg/sec, meaning that the Huey (1200l default capacity) is empty
after 1.2 seconds, while the Hook (9000l default capacity) has 9 seconds of water/retardant,
and can therefore cover larger areas.

_Miscellaneous_
Players can use Communications→Other→Fire Fighting→Secure Ship to safe the auto-
release trigger and/or intakes.

**Putting out which fires?**
Airtank is designed to put out fires that are controlled by the inferno module. You cannot use
it to douse other fires (e.g. crashed aircraft, cooking off vehicles etc).

**Changing Helicopters and their Capacities**
The airtank module comes pre-configured to be used with a number of helicopters and their
tank capacities. You can define your own set of aircraft and capacities per type by adding a
trigger zone and naming it “airTankSpecs”, and then add attributes and values, one each per
type. This replaces all default types and capacities.

The format is as follows:


**Tyle Name Capacity**
[DCS Type
Name of
aircraft]

Example

UH-1H

```
A number to define the capacity of this type’s flame retardant tank in kg
```
```
Example
```
```
1200
```
Note that you can easily exceed the type’s lifting capability. If you do, players may not be
able a fully filled aircraft of that type.

_5.4.27.2 Dependencies_
Airtank requires _dcsCommon_ and _cfxZones._

Although not required, airtank makes sense only if you also add the _inferno_ module to your
mission.

When using “attachTo:”, the radioMenu module is also required.

_5.4.27.3 Module Configuration_
To configure airtank via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “airtankConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

Default is false
menuName Name of the menu installed into the Communications→Other tree for
the airtank interface.

Defaults to “Firefighting”
actionSound Name of the sound file to play to accompany an action event

Defaults to “none”
blipSound Name of the sound file to play once a second while the drop trigger
(ALT) is armed.

Defaults to actionSound
releaseSound Name of the sound file to play (once per second) when the helicopter
releases flame retardant

Defaults to actionSound
pumpSound Name of the sound file to play (once per second) when the intake
pumps are engaged.


**Name Description**

Defaults to actionSound
attachTo: Name of the radioMainMenu zone that airtank’s player menu attaches
to.

Defaults to <none> - attaches to “Other...”
chatty When enabled, each time that an airtank helicopter takes off, the pilot
receives a reminder to top off their tanks, or (when topped off), a “good
luck” message

```
Defaults to true (pilot is sent off with a message)
```
_5.4.27.4 ME Attributes_
You can place fill stations that can instantly refill a helicopter with flame retardant when they
land inside the zone. To create such a zone, place a trigger zone in ME, and add the
following attributes:

**Name Description
airtank** Marks this zone as an airtank refill zone
The value of this attribute is ignored

**Mandatory**
capacity Maximum amount of Flame retardant that can be stored in this refill
zone.

Defaults to 99999999 (essentially infinite)
amount Amount of flame retardant that is stored in this refill station at the start
of the mission. Once this amount is reduced to zero, aircraft can no
longer be refilled from this refill station.

```
Defaults to the amount of capacity
```
_5.4.27.5 Using the module_
Copy the airtank source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail airtank’s behavior.

Optionally, add refill zones to the map.


### 5.4.28 jtacGrpUI

_5.4.28.1 Description_
When you spawn infantry with a spawnZone, these units can have orders to ‘lase’ (laser-
designate) units. This functionality is automatic and does not require additional modules to
control.

Some missions make extensive use of target lasing (especially by combining spawned laser-
designators with heloTroops’ ability for players to deploy them to high ground with player-
controlled helicopters) for other aircraft to pick off lased units.

In these cases it can become advantageous for players to get an overview of which
deployed designators are lasing which units, and get directed there. jtacGrpUI provides a
simple GUI for players to access this information.

**WARNING**
jtacGrpUI _requires_ single-unit player groups to work correctly.

##### WHAT IT DOES

jtacGrpUI provides players with an on-demand report from all units that are currently lasing
targets and that have been spawned by DML spawners (spawnZones) with ‘lase’ orders.
Pilots can access this report and use the information provided to home in on lased targets.

The report is customizable by the mission designer – you can for example display target
locations in MGRS, bearing from calling pilot etc.

##### NOTE:

This ability is different, and works completely independent of, DCS’s JTAC nine-line function.

##### REPORT AND WILDCARDS

The default report lists all groups that are ordered to lase targets. By default, each line of the
report is structured like this:

<name of group> <location> lasing <type of unit lased> [<distance>, <bearing> to player],
code <laser code>

Mission designers can change this by using wildcards similar to the wildcards that are used
in other modules:

Each line is split in two parts: _who_ is lasing (referencing the unit that is lasing the target) and
_what_ they are lasing (referencing the unit that is lased). If the group is not currently lasing a
target, the _what_ part is replaced by “: no target”

Mission designers can use the following wildcards to set up report:


- <u> : name of the unit
- <g> : name of the group
- <p> : name of player controlling that unit (only when that unit is commanded by a
    player using Combined Arms, “AI” otherwise)
- <typ> : type of the unit
- <c> or <C> : coalition of that unit (lower and UPPER case)
- <e> or <E> : enemy coalition for that unit (lower and UPPER case)
- <twn> : name of closest town on map to that unit (requires ‘twn’ module)
- <twnkm> : name, distance (in km) and direction from nearest town (requires ‘twn’
    module)
- <twnnm> : name, distance (in nautical miles) and direction from nearest town
    (requires ‘twn’ module)
- <lat> : latitude of unit
- <lon> : longitude of unit
- <elem> : elevation of unit in meters
- <eleft> : elevation of unit in feet
- <bea> : bearing of unit to player requesting report
- <rng> : range (in km) of unit to player requesting report
- <rngnm> : range (in nm) of unit to player requesting report
- <code> : laser code used to lase target

By default, jtacGrpUI sets _who_ and _what_ up to

- Who: <g><twnnm>
- What: - lasing <typ> [<rngnm>nm, bearing <bea>°], code=<code>

Which will result in reports similar to

##### WHO HAS ACCESS

By default, all players have access to the jtac report. Mission designers can restrict this to
certain classes (e.g. Planes). jtacGrpUI supports the following classes

- All or Any (all players)
- Planes (fixed-wing only)
- Helo or Helicopters (rotor-wing only)

**Note:**
jtacGrpUI supports players in ground vehicles using Combined Arms. If you limit access to
the report menu, players in ground vehicles will not be able to access the report.

_5.4.28.2 Dependencies_
jtacGrpUI requires _dcsCommon_ , _cfxZones_ and _cfxGroundTroops_


Although not required, jtacGrpUI can take advantage of the _twn_ module.

If you want to use the _attachTo:_ ability, you must include the _radioMenu_ module

_5.4.28.3 Module Configuration_
To configure jtacGrpUI via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “jtacGrpUIConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

Default is false
jtacTypes Unit types that are eligible to get access to the jtac reports

Defaults to “All”
jtacSound Name of soundfile to play when report is generated

Defaults to <none>
attachTo: Name of the radioMainMenu zone for hierarchical menus
Requires the radioMenu module when used.

Defaults to <none>.
menuName Name of the menu that requests reports.

Defautls to “jtac Lasing Report”
who “Left side” of the report, who is lasing. Supports wildcards, referenced
unit is the unit lasing

Defaults to "<g><twnnm>", e.g. “JTAC Hill 1.2km NE of Batumi”
what “Right side” of the report, target that is being lased. Supports wildcards.
Referenced unit is the one being lased.

```
Defaults to " - lasing <typ> [<rngnm>nm, bearing <bea>°],
code=<code>", e.g. “ - lasing AAV [9.7nm, bearing 313°],
code 1688”
```
_5.4.28.4 ME Attributes_
jtacGrpGUI has no mission runtime interface, only config

_5.4.28.5 Using the module_
Copy the jtacGrpUI source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail jtacGrpUI’s behavior.


#### 5.4.29 planeGuard

_5.4.29.1 Description_
The _planeGuard_ module is a tiny helper module to facilitate some eye-candy in your
missions and is solely targeted to make missions more immersive: it adds helicopters on
‘plane guard’ duty to naval units. These helicopters are intended to protect and rescue pilots
and seamen who – for one reason or another – fall into the drink.

These helicopters are most prominent around carriers, but they can be spotted around other
naval vessels as well.

The planeGuard module simply makes this process very easy, experienced mission creators
can achieve the same using standard ME tools and a lot of patience. With a planeGuard
zone you can do the same in seconds.

Note that these helicopters are for visual effect only, they do not perform SAR or other duty
should the need arise, they simply accompany the naval unit that they perform plane guard
for. They do not maneuver, they don’t even refuel (using a special ‘eternal avgas’ brand only
available in DCS).

##### ADDING A PLANE GUARD

In order to add a plane guard helicopter to any naval unit, place a trigger zone close to the
naval unit at the location where you want the helicopter to be stationed. The zone
automatically attaches itself to the nearest naval unit, and performs all calculations required
for the helicopter to remain positioned relative to the naval unit – even when it moves or
turns. If there are no naval units in the mission, the module complains with a message at
mission start.

All plane guard helicopters are created at mission start. Only helicopters can perform plane
guard.

##### SUPPORTED HELICOPTERS

planeGuard supports all helicopters that are supported by DCS, including mods.

##### NO REFUEL NEEDED

planeGuard helicopters never run out of fuel, so there is no need to replenish their fuel
reserves.


_5.4.29.2 Dependencies_
planeGuard requires dcsCommon, cfxZones and cfxMX.

_5.4.29.3 Module Configuration_
To configure planeGuard via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “planeGuardConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

```
Default is “false”
```
_5.4.29.4 ME Attributes_
**Name Description
planeGuard** Tells DML that this zone is a planeGuard zone.
The zone (and helicopter) attach to the **nearest naval unit** , with
distance and offset at mission start defining the offset to be kept during
the entire mission.

```
The value of this attribute defines what helicopter to use. You must
provide a DCS typeName as defined here:
https://github.com/mrSkortch/DCS-
miscScripts/tree/master/ObjectDB/Helicopter
```
```
The helicopter automatically assumes the coalition of the naval unit that
it attaches to.
```
```
Defaults to CH-53E (Super Stallion)
```
**Mandatory**
alt The altitude at which the plane guard flight is performed.

```
Defaults to 40m (120 feet)
```
_5.4.29.5 Using the module_
Copy the planeGuard source into a DOSCRIPT action that runs at the start of the mission.

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail planeGuard’s behavior.

Add one or more trigger zones with a ‘planeGuard’ attribute to your mission. Place these
zones near and at the location relative to the naval units where the plane guard duties
should commence.


### 5.5 “Compound” modules

Just like LEGO® provides some special, purpose-built parts that can only be used for some
very specific needs, DML provides some compound modules that integrate the functionality
of other modules to build a bigger, better part.

#### 5.5.1 FireCtrl

_5.5.1.1 Description_
Building endless FireFigphting missions becomes easy with fireCtrol. Its goal is to marshal
the abilities of airtank and Inferno to provide players with a seamless and fun experience as
well as scorekeeping/ranking.

##### STANDARD ABILITIES

**Keep the fires coming**
FireCtrl makes sure that there are always a minimal number of inferno (fire cells) burning.
This of course requires that you distribute enough inferno zones on the map. FireCtrl
randomizes selection and tells the chosen inferno zones to start their fire(s). That way, your
mission never runs out of fires to fight, making it fir perfectly for endless server-hosted
sandbox missions.

**... unless not enabled**
The fireCtrl module can be enabled and disabled while the mission is running. When turned
off (disabled), no additional fires are started. If FireCtrl is off at mission start, no fires are
started until it is turned on.

Note also that when fireCtrl is turned off, it suppresses the welcome messages to airtank
helicopters.

**Keep the Score / Rank**
Players are identified by their name, and their contribution to fires is recorded and rewarded
with points. FireCtrl then awards a rank based on the points earned. For example, a player
with 0 points has the rank of “Probie’, while at a later stage (and score) they may be given
the rank of “Chief”

**Supports Persistence**
When configured accordingly (and DCS set up to allow persistence), FireCtrl automatically
uses persistence to save score when persistence is triggered.


**Optional Player UI**
When enabled, FireCtrol provides players (in airtank-compatible aircraft) menus to guide
them to the nearest fires and to check up on their score

**Sound Effects**
FireCtrl allows you to supply sound files that can accompany many events. If you don not
supply sound files, there merely is no aural feedback.

_5.5.1.2 Dependencies_
FireCtrl requires the following modules

- dcsCommon
- cfxZones
- airtank
- inferno

Optionally, it also supports persistence

_5.5.1.3 Module Configuration_
Since FireCtrol serves as a module integrator, most of its aspects are controlled by a config
zone.

To configure fireCtrl via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “planeGuardConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

Default is “false”
ups The number of checks that fireCtrl performs per second. Use a small
fraction of 1, for example 0.03 to perform one check every 30 seconds.

Defaults to 0.02 to perform one check every 20 seconds
actionSound Name of the sound file to play whenever something noteworthy (from
fireCtrl’s point of view) happens. This can significantly upgrade the
experience of the fire-fighting aspect of your mission.
Note that many other configurable sounds default to this setting, so you
can supply a single sound file to upgrade your sound experience

Defaults to <none>
listSound Name of sound file to play when fireCtrl displays a list

Defaults to _actionSound_
scoreSound Name of the sound file to play then the player requests the scores list

Defaults to _listSound_
newFire Name of the sound file to play whenever fireCtrl tells inferno zones to


**Name Description**
start a new fire.

Defaults to _actionSound_
centerName Name of the fire control authority that appears in some text messages.

Defaults to “Highperch”
menuName Name of the menu that is installed in player’s menus to access fireCtrl’s
UI (when enabled)

Defaults to “Contact <centerName>”
minFires Number of fires (inferno zones) that should always be burning at the
same time. Requires that you have at least that many inferno zones on
your map.

Defaults to 3
sparks When the number of active infernos drops below _minFires_ , fireCtrl can
start 1 to _sparks_ new fires. This helps to randomize the mission.

Defaults to 3
notifications When set to true, new fires are announced by command authority.

Defaults to true (new fires are announced)
UI When set to true, fireCtrl provides players a UI to get directions to the
nearest fires and call up their scores

Defaults to true (players have access to UI)
onStart If set to true, fireCtrl starts up immediately, else it requires a command
on the ctrOn? Input.

Defaults to true, fireCtrl becomes active immediately
ctrOn? Input to turn on (enable) fireCtrl. When turned on, fireCtrl ensures that
_minFire_ number of fires are always burning.

Defaults no <none>
ctrOff? Input to turn off (disable) fireCtrl. When turned off, no new fires are
started, even if the number of fires drops below _minFires_.

Defaults to <none>
method DML method that triggers inputs.

```
Defaults to “change”
```
_5.5.1.4 ME Attributes_
Currently, fireCtrl only uses config zones


_5.5.1.5 Using the module_
Copy the airtank, inferno and fireCtrl source into DOSCRIPT actions that run at the start of
the mission.

Add airtank and inferno zones as per those modules’ documentation

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail fireCtrl’s behavior.


#### 5.5.2 csarFX

_5.5.2.1 Description_
This module provides additional services/adornments for the _csarManager_ module to make
(C)SAR missions more life-like and/or challenging. It allows to clearly delineate between
CSAR (combat) and SAR (unconflicted) missions by supporting additional attributes in
CSAR zones.

_csarFX_ can provide additional services/elements to a mission that uses _csarManager_ to
enact SAR and CSAR missions, such as:

- Debris/wrecks close to the evacuee
    o Different types of wrecks depending on the terrain type (road, open terrain,
       water) where the evacuee is on
    o When the evacuee is to be rescued from a road, _csarFX_ can even create a
       road accident of multiple vehicles
- It can place enemy units close to the location of the evacuee
- It can place special, more dangerous enemies close to the location of the evacuee
- It can cause previously placed enemies around the evacuee to congregate on the
    position of the evacuee as soon as they pop smoke

csarFX integrates with _csarManager_ automatically. Whenever _csarManager_ creates a new
CSAR mission after _csarFX_ loads, it automatically invokes _csarFX_. This means that missions
created at startup of _csarManager_ will not be able to access the services of _csarFX_. Many of
_csarFX_ ’s abilities are controlled by extensions (additional attributes) to _csarManager_ ’s CSAR
zones.

**Debris / Wrecks close to the evacuee**

_csarFX_ can place a wreck (in DCS, ‘wrecks’ are static objects that have their ‘dead’ attribute
set. Sadly, not all static objects in DCS have a dead/destroyed model, so some ‘wrecks’ can
look like pristine static objects) close to the location of the evacuee.

Unless there are enemies to populate the evacuee site (see below), _csarFX_ chooses the
debris / wrecks depending on the land type that the evacuee landed on. Currently, _csarFX_
knows the following land types

- **In populated/city areas** (taken from the ‘inBuiltup’ attribute in CSAR zone):
    Add a fire/smoke with 50:50 probability. No wrecks or debris (area is assumed to be


```
heavily built up without space for wrecks)
```
- **deep water** (taken from evacuee’s position)
    There is a 50:50 chance that _csarFX_ adds an object from the _seaTypes_ collection of
    water objects.
    There is also a 1% chance that _csarFX_ adds an Orca in the vicinity. Orcas are
    harmless and must not be attacked.
- **Road** (taken from the evacuee’s position)
    There is a 50:50 chance that _csarFX_ adds a two-vehicle crash else single vehicle
    accident (with a high probability of fire) as scenery adornment. The vehicles are
    taken from _roadTypes_. Note that CSAR zones can force the evacuee to be
    positioned on a road by setting “ _onRoad_ ” to true-
- **Open Land** (taken from evacuees position)
    There is a 50:50 chance that some debris is taken from _landTypes_ , and some chance
    that fire/smoke is added.

**CSAR – enemies around the evacuee (turns SAR into CSAR)**

If the CSAR zone has an _enemies_ attribute, the SAR mission turns into a CSAR mission with
enemy forces placed in close proximity to the evacuee. You must take care not to place the
enemies too close to the evacuee (see _range_ attribute), else the evacuee is captured
immediately. When enemies are active, above _Debris/Wrecks_ functionality is skipped, and
the following can happen instead:

- Strength (randomizable) units of type _enemies_ (randomized) are placed range
    (randomizable) meters from the evacuee. Choose the minimal range carefully, so
    they do not immediately engage the evacuee.
- A (one) wreck is picked from the _debris_ attribute and placed in some distance (1 km)
    to the evacuee. There’s some probability of flames and/or smoke coming out of the
    wreck.
- When present, one unit is taken from the _nasties_ attribute and placed range
    (randomized) kilometers somewhere around the evacuee.

_csarFX_ differentiates between ‘ _enemies_ ’ and ‘ _nasties_ ’ so that you can place **one** especially
dangerous unit into the mix. Note that you cannot place _nasties_ without _enemies_.

**Now we’re cooking: attracting enemies**

When enemies are placed around the evacuee and the SAR has turned into a CSAR, there’s
an additional ability _csarFX_ can use to increase pressure: as soon as the evacuee on the
ground pops smoke, _csarFX_ can tell all the units to congregate on that smoke. To do so,
enable _csarFX_ ’s _makeEnemiesCongregate_ attribute in the config zone.

_5.5.2.2 Dependencies_
csarFX requires dcsCommon, cfxZones and csarManager.

Note that csarManager may require additional modules to run.


_5.5.2.3 Module Configuration_
To configure csarFX via a configuration zone,

- Place a Trigger Zone anywhere on the map
- Name it “csarFXConfig” (note: name _must_ match exactly)
- Add any of the following attributes to this zone:

**Name Description**
verbose A value of “true” turns on debugging messages

Default is “false”
congregateOnSmoke If set to true, enemies start to converge on the evacuee when the
evacuee pops smoke. Note that this requires the enemies attribute
be present for the CSAR zone.

Defaults to true, enemies will congregate in CSAR missions
landTypes List (comma separated) of DCS Type names from which csarFX can
choose to place decorative, destroyed 3D models close to the
evacuee that stands on a generic land type of terrain. Note that DCS
often does not provide a destroyed version of a model, so the result
can look either generic or pristine (undamaged).
Defaults to _Hummer_ (yeah, just a single Hummer)
roadTypes List (comma separated) of DCS Type names from which csarFX can
choose to place decorative, destroyed 3D models close to the
evacuee that stands on a road type of terrain (note that this can be
enforced with a CSAR zone’s _onRoad_ attribute). When the evacuee
is located on a road, csarFX can also arrange for a vehicle accident,
by colliding two entries from this list.
Note that DCS often does not provide a destroyed version of a
model, so the result can look either generic or pristine (unsamaged).
Defaults to
_VAZ Car, IKARUS Bus, LAZ Bus, LiAZ Bus, GAZ-3307, ZIL- 131
KUNG, MAZ-6303, ZIL-4331, Ural-375, GAZ-3307, ZIL-131 KUNG,
MAZ-6303, ZIL-4331, Ural-375, GAZ-3307, VAZ Car, P20_01,
TugHarlan, r11_volvo_drivable_
seaTypes List (comma separated) of DCS Type names from which csarFX can
choose to place decorative objects close to the evacuee that swims
in deep water.
Defaults to _speedboat, ZWEZDNY speedboat_
(“speedboat” is listed **twice** to make it twice as likely to be picked
over “Zwezdny”)
cleanupDelay Number of seconds after which all units and objects (this includes all
enemies) are deallocated after an evacuee is picked up, expires or is
killed.

_5.5.2.4 ME Attributes_
**Warning!** _csarFX_ extends “ _CSAR_ ” triggerZones utilized by _csarManager_! All attributes listed
below must be inside a trigger zone with the _CSAR_ attribute (→ _csarManager_ )


**Name Description
CSAR** Marks this zone as a csarManager mission destination
Please see csarManager documentation

**Mandatory**
enemies Turns a SAR into a CSAR – csarFX picks a number of enemies (see
_strength_ attribute, below) and places them some distance (see range
_attribute_ , below) from the evacuee.
The value of this attribute are a comma-separated list of DCS type
names from which csarFX picks randomly. You can increase the
likelihood of a certain type being chosen by repeating it.
When csarFX’s _congregateOnSmoke_ attribute is set to true, all enemies
start to move towards the smoke when it is popped.
Example: _Soldier M4, Soldier RPG, Soldier AK_

```
Note:
the number of entries in this list does not influence the number of
enemies that are placed around the evacuee – use the strength
attribute for that
```
Defaults to <none> - unconflicted SAR mission.
nasties This can turn a CSAR into a difficult CSAR by adding a dangerous unit
to the mix. The value of this attribute is a comma-separated list of DCS
type names from which csarFX randomly picks one to add to the
enemies (see above) around the evacuee.
Since CSAR-capable helicopters often can’t combat well-armored
vehicles, be careful which types you choose.

```
Example: Stinger manpad GRG, tt_KORD
```
```
Note:
nasties are only added if enemies are defined.
```
Defaults to <none>
strength Defines how many _enemies_ (above) are placed around the evacuee.
Can be a range (e.g. 3-7) to randomize troop strength.

Defaults to 1
range Distance _in kilometers_ of the enemies (see above) that are placed
around the evacuee. Can be a range (e.g. 1-2) to randomize distance.
Be careful to make the range far enough so that the enemies do not
immediately engage the evacuee

Defaults to 1 (km)
debris A comma-separated list of DCS type names from which csarFX picks
one that will be placed ca. 1 km away from the evacuee to simulate a
downed or destroyed unit.
Example: _A-10A, F-16C bl.50, F-15E, AH-1W, UH-1H, UH-60A_

```
Defaults to <none>
```

_5.5.2.5 Using the module_
Copy the csarManager (and it’s dependend modules) and csarFX source into DOSCRIPT
actions that run at the start of the mission. Make sure that csarManager loads before
csarFX, and all required zones for csarManager (see that module’s documentation)

Add CSAR zones as per csarManager’s documentation, and amend these zones with
attributes from csarFX

Optionally, add a config zone and curtail csarFX’s behavior.


#### 5.5.3 Module Name

_5.5.3.1 Description
5.5.3.2 Dependencies_

_5.5.3.3 Module Configuration_

_5.5.3.4 ME Attributes
5.5.3.5 Using the module_


### 5.6 Server-based modules

Some modules in DML can interface with scripts that are installed on the server side of DCS,
meaning that these modules usually only work when such a mission is run as multi-player.

For example, the module “ssbClient” interfaces with a popular server script (Ciribob’s “simple
slot block”, “SSB”). If that SSB script is not installed on the hosting server, or the mission is
run in single-player mode (i.e. it’s not run un a server), the mission module (ssbClient) will
simply have no effect.

Other DML modules provide additional/strengthened stability to server-based missions, while
other modules (e.g. “Tickle”) may even provide exclusive, server-only ability that aren’t
available in single-player mode.

This section of the documentation describes the DML server modules: what they do, and
how they communicate with a mission.

##### NOTE

In order to understand the inner details discussed in sections of this chapter, it helps if you
understand how DCS server is set up, and how the server communicates with its clients.


#### 5.6.1 smrGUI

_5.6.1.1 Description_
This tiny server modules allows a mission to re-start the mission simply by setting a flag to
the value larger than 0

This is helpful for mission designers who incorporate the mission’s version into the name
(e.g. “my cool mission 1.2.7.miz”) because a mission currently cannot easily restart itself. By
installing the server module, a mission merely needs to change a flag, and the server
module re-starts the mission.

This is also helpful during debugging sessions when you quickly want to re-start the mission
without having to go through a mission editor cycle: Simply use the debugger (or some ME-
built trigger) to trigger the restart.

_5.6.1.2 Mission Interaction_
smr watches the mission’s flag space and reacts to changes in the following flags:

**Flag Description**
simpleMissionRestart When this flag has a value other than 0, smrGUI restarts the
mission.

_5.6.1.3 Using the module_
The module must be installed in */Saved Games/DCS/Scripts/Hooks


#### 5.6.2 stopGapGUI

_5.6.2.1 Description_
stopGapGUI provides some important functionality for the stopGap module on the server to
work around a DCS synchronization bug. stopGapGUI has next to no performance impact
and does not interfere with any other server module so you can simply install it on your
server.

If your players complain that their aircraft are falling from the sky when they enter them, DCS
is exhibiting the above-mentioned synchronization bug, and stopGapGUI is not active.

_5.6.2.2 Mission Interaction_
stopGapGUI provides required synchronization information for stopGap to prevent player
aircraft from falling out of the sky when players enter the cockpit.

_5.6.2.3 Using the module_
The module must be installed in */Saved Games/DCS/Scripts/Hooks


#### 5.6.3 noGapGUI

_5.6.3.1 Description_
Like stopGapGUI, noGapGUI provides some important functionality for the noGap module
on the server to work around a DCS synchronization bug. noGapGUI has next to no
performance impact and does not interfere with any other server module so you can simply
install it on your server.

If your players complain that their aircraft are falling from the sky when they enter them, DCS
is exhibiting the above-mentioned synchronization bug, and noGapGUI is not active.

_5.6.3.2 Mission Interaction_
noGapGUI provides required synchronization information for noGap to prevent player aircraft
from falling out of the sky when players enter the cockpit.

_5.6.3.3 Using the module_
The module must be installed in */Saved Games/DCS/Scripts/Hooks


#### 5.6.4 Tickle (tcliGUI)

_5.6.4.1 Description_
“Tickle” is a tiny CLI (“Command-Line Interface” – the most primitive, yet also most efficient
way to tell a computer what to do) that allows Eagle Dynamic’s DCS servers to

- _automatically rotate_ missions in your 'server playlist' on a time basis (e.g. after 4
    hours) and
- allow _specially authorized_ players to interactively (via mission chat) change the
    currently running mission

In other words, you can set up your server ‘play list’ with 5 missions, set the cycle time to 2
hours, and then the server changes the active mission every 2 hours to another mission from
that play list. This can help you to keep your server ‘fresh’ for your players by serving up
ever-changing missions automatically. For example, I set up a ‘helicopter’ themed server to
cycle between six helicopter rescue sandboxes and change the active mission every 90
minutes.

Also, server owners (or players who are authorized) can change the currently playing
mission simply by issuing a chat command (this requires the authorized person be on-line
and inside the game).

**Note:**
Tickle is a server enhancement and only works in DCS multiplayer / dedicated server.


_5.6.4.2 Mission Interaction_
Tickle provides you (and all privileged users that you have added to the config file) with
interactive chat commands that control the currently running mission.

**Note** :
All Tickle commands start with a hyphen "-".

To issue a command in a mission, enter chat as authorized player, and type in a command
as listed below:

- -?
    quick help and current automatic mission rotation status
- - next
    run the next mission in the server mission playlist. Loops. Ignores current shuffle
    setting
- - prev or -previous
    run previous mission from playlist. Loops. Ignores current shuffle setting
- - restart
    re-start this mission
- - random
    run a random mission from your server mission playlist
- - pause
    pauses the current mission. This stops mission time
- - play
    un-pauses the current mission. This continues mission time
- - shuffle
    Turns on mission shuffling, randomizing the next mission when the cycleTime timer
    runs down
- - seq or -sequence
    Turns off shuffling of missions when the cycleTime timer runs down.
- - cycleTime
    **the big kahuna (Tickle’s** **_raison d'être_** **)** : automatic mission rotation See “Automatic
    Mission Rotation”, below
- - cycle
    Immediately cycle to the next mission (observes shuffle settings)

Whenever you (or any other authorized user) give Tickle a command in DCS chat, it is **not**
broadcast, and only you receive any answers. This is intentional: for added security, nobody
can see who gives what commands to Tickle.


_5.6.4.3 Using the module_

5.6.4.3.1 Installation
Installation is once-only, and drag-and-drop:

1. Put the file tcliGUI.lua into your DCS/Scripts/Hooks folder
2. Restart DCS

You now have installed Tickle on your computer. Its functions are only available in multi-
player (local and dedicated server). If you start a multiplayer session now, you won't see any
change because although Tickle is active, it does not yet know you.

If you have a hosting provider for your DCS servers (e.g. Fox3), you may need to have them
put the tclGUI.lua file into the /Hooks folder for you.

5.6.4.3.2 Authorizing players (interactive mode)
You need to tell Tickle who you are, and that it should accept your orders.

To do so, look into your
**/Missions** directory and locate
the file tcli.config (see the
yellow markings in the image to
the right). **It automatically
appears in your /Missions as
soon as DCS starts up after
you install Tickle.** This config
file contains the information that
Tickle needs to recognize you (and other people whom you trust enough to control your
server’s mission sequence).

Use a text editing tool like "Notepad" or "Notepad++" to open _tcli.config_. There'll be some
gibberish text that looks like this:

There are two silly privileged usernames pre-configured:


- xk76hkl@01 (marked yellow)
- %tgJsgRG1< (marked pink)

Replace one of them (or both) with **your DCS on-line user name** , and save the file. My
DCS username is (obviously) CFRAG, and so, to authorize myself (and leave the second
name untouched) on my DCS server install, my tcli.config file looks like this:

i.e. I replaced xk76hkl@01 with CFRAG (you **must** leave the quotes ( **“** ) before and after the
name). Save the config file and start a new server mission. Note that unlike creaky old DCS,
Tickle notices, and loads your changes. It does **not** require you to restart DCS, all you need
to do is start a new multiplayer mission. As soon as the server loads a new mission, Tickle
reloads tcli.config from your /Missions folder. If you changed the config file correctly,
Tickle now recognizes you as an authorized player.

Let’s try this. Enter the mission, open a chat window and enter the command

"-?" (without the quotes, return at the end)

If all went well, DCS responds (only to
you) with a quick help screen that lists
all commands, and tells you that
currently, automatic mission changes
are turned off.

If you don't see that response, you will have to re-edit the config file, save, and start a new
multiplayer mission to reload tcli.config. Note that **usernames are case-sensitive** and
therefore you must spell your DCS username exactly in the config file for Tickle to recognize
you.

If you do not yet know your DCS username (it’s the one you use to log into DCS), look at the
chat window after you have typed a message. Your message always begins with your
username.

Mine is CFRAG, all caps. Subtle, I know.

5.6.4.3.3 Automatic mission rotation
One of Tickle’s main features is that it can automatically rotate missions on your server after
some time (e.g. 2 hours) elapses. This feature is mainly for dedicated servers that run
"endless" sandbox-type missions to automatically rotate though a roster of missions and
provide greater variety to players. Tickle uses your server's mission roster and respects the
"shuffle" flag (meaning: if shuffle is enabled in your server mission list, Tickle chooses
missions in random order, else they are chosen in sequence).


To turn on automatic mission rotation, issue the command -cycleTime and supply the
number of minutes each cycle lasts. For example:

- cycleTime 240

tells Tickle that it should change the currently running every 240 minutes (=4 hours).

**Tickle automatically saves your cycleTime settings to storage and keeps them
between DCS restarts.** When you issue the -cycleTime <amount> command, that time
interval is measured against the time when the mission started (i.e. it includes the time that
already has passed in the current mission). So, if you are in hour 3 of your mission, and you
give a cycle time of 120 (=2 hours), the mission cycles immediately.

To find out how much time is left in a cycle, issue the -cycleTime command without any
parameters, e.g.

- cycleTime

If cycleTime is active and nearing the end, there is a warning broadcast 5 and 1 minutes
before the mission changes.

**To turn off** mission rotation, provide a cycleTime of 0 (zero), e.g.

- cycleTime 0

You can always check on the current cycle status by either giving the '-?' command or telling
tcli '-cycleTime' (without a number).

**Note:**
Tickle automatically rotates to the next mission after sufficient **MISSION TIME** elapses. A
_paused_ mission freezes mission time, so when a server is set to automatically pause after
the last client leaves, that server **will not rotate** missions unless clients return and the time
finally runs down with players present. Set your server to "Resume on Load" to ensure that
your server continues to run the mission after the last client has disconnected.

To rotate now, issue the -cycle chat command from within the mission.

5.6.4.3.4 Randomizing Missions
If you are local/ad-hoc-hosting your missions, you have the
option to turn on ‘shuffling’ of missions on your playlist. If you


enable that option, Tickle automatically randomizes the next mission whenever cycleTime
runs down.

**WARNING:**
Some of ED’s GUI people appear to possess lamentably low UX skills. In the current ad-hoc
server GUI metaphor, a _white_ symbol signifies that the option is _on_ (enabled), while a blue
symbol means that it is off, disabled. It’s just the way DCS rolls, I guess.

Some dedicated server web GUIs may not provide this option, making it inaccessible to
dedicated servers.

Tickle to the rescue: you can interactively use the “-shuffle” and “-sequence”
commands to turn on and disable shuffling, respectively. This is also saved to the server’s
configuration, so it will persist through server restarts, and of course also works for local/ad-
hoc-hosted missions (here the GUI will reflect the current shuffle setting if you changed it
interactively)

To turn off shuffling, issue the “-seq” or “-sequence” command interactively (or use the
GUI for local/ad-hoc hosted missions)

5.6.4.3.5 Advanced Uses
Tickle supports multiple (unlimited even) privileged users. By default, Tickle provides you
with two users in its tcli.config file:

["xk76hkl@01","%tgJsgRG1<"]

Inside those square brackets you can change the "names" inside the quotes. And you can
add many more, simply by adding a comma "," and another name in quotes for each. For
example, this is how the config looks if it was to support four users named "Iceman",
"DingHe", "Suppa" and "Lil' me":

{"mark":"-","admins":["Iceman","DingHe","Suppa","Lil' me"],"cycleTime":-1}


```
cf/x Dynamic Mission Library
for DCS
```
# PART IV:

# PERSISTENCE


## 6 Persistence

This section deals with an optional DML feature called ‘persistence’. Standard DCS
installations require that you actively disable security features to enable persistence. Many
players do not feel comfortable with either the process of disabling these features, or the
idea of playing with lessened security, or both. Therefore, I recommend that you think
carefully before you add persistence to your mission. That being said, using persistence you
can do incredibly interesting and fun things that are impossible to achieve otherwise.

**Important Note:**
As of April 2025, DCS is phasing in a new feature that is called “Save State” or “Save
Mission” that aims to accomplish similar goals as DML’s persistence feature, i.e. allowing
players to save a mission at one point, and pick up and continue from that point later.

This functionality in DCS is experimental, and, from a player’s perspective, not usable at all.
At the time of this writing, the DCS “Save State” abilities appear to make DCS a worse
experience, and **I strongly recommend against using it**. Using DCS’s “Save State” feature
does not work with DML.

As the situation improves, and DCS’s “Save State” function approaches a tenable state,
DML will integrate with DCS’s persistence ability, as that is clearly good choice.

### 6.1 What is persistence?

Simply put, persistence is the ability to read and/or write data to permanent store, so when a
mission writes data and then ends, that data can be retrieved later and acted upon: the data
persists past the end of the process. In other words, persistence is often used for:

- Saving and later continuing a mission (in DCS to a limited degree)
- Keeping records (scores, kills, departures, landings)
- Sharing data among different applications (or missions).
- Exporting data for other applications

This is an incredibly empowering ability for mission designers, as it theoretically allows a
mission creator to write a string of missions that take into account the results or some main
feature from the previous mission (say, units that were destroyed in the previous mission
remain destroyed in the follow-up mission, making it more difficult or less difficult depending
on the player’s past performance), and integrate it, creating the illusion of theater
consistency.

So, why is persistency disabled by default and requires a non-trivial effort for the player to
enable?

**Security**. Read on.

#### 6.1.1 FOR YOUR OWN PROTECTION: READ THIS CAREFULLY

If a mission script has unfettered access to your storage, it can achieve a lot of great things.
On the other hand, at the hands of a malicious mind, **it can also create a lot of damage**.


Lua’s – and therefore DCS’s – only model of storage access is “all or nothing”. There are
other, more granular, firewalled approaches that mitigate the risk of giving access to storage
to applications (e.g. Apple’s Application Sandbox for iOS and macOS), but they are not
available to mission designers in DCS. Since missions run as part of DCS, this means that
you can either give a mission full access to your entire storage, or no access at all. So, if
DCS gave full storage access to all Lua scripts, it will only take a few hours until the first
maliciously crafted missions appear that exploit file access for nefarious purposes, with
vandalism and blackmail being some of the _lesser_ evils.

Therefore, by default, mission scripts can’t just willy-nilly write to your storage, you must
explicitly instruct DCS to allow this. **That is good, as it protects casual players and
mission designers who import a mission from the internet with malicious content
from the damage that such a mission could cause.** This works well and makes DCS
more secure.

The result, on the other hand, is that mission designers who want to write some data to file
must actively disable these safety features before their mission scripts can write to storage.
Again, this is a good thing, as it does not involve simply clicking a dialog (something a
person may be socially engineered to do), but a more involved process that requires you to
open and edit a file in DCS’s main installation. In other words: If you allow DCS to write to
your storage, that does not happen by accident.

More importantly, it also requires that anyone who runs (hosts) a mission that uses
persistence must first disable these security features (this is only true for single-player
missions that want to use persistence, and those people who self-host multiplayer missions.
If you log on to a multiplayer game that uses persistence, your DCS does _not_ have to
persistence-enabled, all data is persisted on the server, never on your client).

Accordingly, if you author a mission that uses/requires persistence, be aware that this can
significantly reduce the enthusiasm towards your mission from potential players. As a rule of
thumb (and in DCS’s current model of nothing/all access), persistency may be a good idea
for long multiplayer missions and server missions that share player scores. It should be
avoided for single-player missions, or be entirely optional.

#### 6.1.2 De-Sanitizing LFS and IO

**There are legitimate, even desirable reasons to allow a mission to be able to write to
storage** : to save and restore a mission, to manage high scores that persist through mission
re-starts, to maintain a log of griefers, etc.

To use DML’s persistence, **you (and anyone who hosts the mission that you are
authoring) must** therefore perform the steps below to **“de-sanitize” two security features**
that will remove restrictions place on Lua by DCS:

- Go to your main DCS installation ( _not_ the saved games folder, we aren’t in Kansas
    any more)
- Open the folder named “Scripts”
- Look for the file “MissionScripting.lua”
- **Back up this file**
- Open the original
- Look for “sanitizeModule('io')”
- Place two dashes in front so it looks like this: “-- sanitizeModule('io')”


- Look for “sanitizeModule('lfs')”
- Place two dashes in front so it looks like this: “-- sanitizeModule('lfs')”
- Save and close the file, **restart DCS**

For reference, the block of code inside ‘MissionScripting.lua’ should look like this:

sanitizeModule('os')
**-- sanitizeModule('io')
-- sanitizeModule('lfs')**
_G['require'] = nil
_G['loadlib'] = nil
_G['package'] = nil

So, what have you done? By placing the two dashes in front of the ‘sanitizeModule()’
