tells scribe that this data is to be
reported (default is false), and with
_lRescues = RSQ:_ (note the colon “:” at
the end!) we set the new column’s name to be “RSQ:”.


Behind the scenes, Scribe fetches the rescue data from csarManager automatically, this
does not require anything from you to enable.

So, on the surface, this concludes our discussion of this mission that shows you how to use
scribe in your mission, and get it to report your helicopter rescues.

Now, if you are interested in the really cool stuff, read the Discussion.

### 8.82.3 Discussion

**Wait... ALL Helicopters?**
The first thing to mention is something that you may only have discovered if you had tried to
play this mission in a helicopter that is not supported by csarManager out-of-the-box. Here,
suddenly, csarManager supports _all_ helicopters, even some types that DCS doesn’t know
about (for example the user-created Blackhawk). This is due to the fact that I added a config
zone for csarManager:

The _troopCarriers_ attribute can be used
to tell csarManager which units can
perform rescue missions, and setting it
to the special _“helos”_ keyword allows all
helicopters to perform them.

**Don’t be a downer...**
There’s another attribute here: “addPrefix = no” – it is used to shorten an evacuees name.
By default, csarManager would prepend ‘Downed’ to a pilot’s name, since the most common
use case for csarManager is to rescue downed pilots. Here, we omit that and only show the
names of the people to rescue, without the ‘downed’ prefix.

**Jessie Powell? Ferdinant Barker? Nice names!**
If you continue rescuing people you will
notice that each mission sports their
own evacuee name, and (unless you
are _really_ dedicated) they don’t repeat, yet sound realistic. csarManager uses the ‘random
names’ feature here that accesses the **names** module to generate (mostly western) names
from a database of common names and surnames that can generate up to 360’000 unique
names. We turn on this feature by setting _*rnd_ as value for the _csar_ attribute.

**Wow, those missions surely are conveniently placed on roads...**


After rescuing a couple of evacuees you will have noticed that the people that you need to
evacuate are all placed nicely accessible on a road. This is no coincidence and controlled
with the “ _onRoad = true_ ” attribute for the CSAR missions. This option is incredibly helpful if
you want to create accessible (in a very literal way) randomly placed missions (remember
that CSAR missions default to random locations inside their zone). In this mission, the
randomized area is a good chunk of Senaki-Kolkhi’s city proper, and if we merely chose a
random location inside the area, there would be a good chance that the location comes to lie
inside a building, making the mission impossible to complete.

Since roads are much better accessible, and (at least on the Caucasus map) wide, telling
csarManager to place evacuees on roads is a good compromise. The downside is that this
only works if the region where you place CSAR missions needs to have roads. Remember to
turn off road traffic lest you evacuee gets run over, or your helicopter become road kill during
the rescue attempt.

**Huh. Only Scribe persists its data?**
This is something so subtle that very few people notice outright: although the mission has
persistence, only Scribe persists its data. This is intentional (of course) and a result of taking
advantage of a persistence rule that is easy to overlook:

A section on persistence in this manual states (even in bold face):

**Persistence is only available to those modules that load after persistence** itself loads.

The reason behind above rule is that modules must sign up
to persistence, and if persistence isn’t loaded when a
module starts up, it can’t sign up. If a module hasn’t signed
up for persistence, it won’t get asked for data to save when
persistence does its thing, and consequentially there is no
data to load when persistence loads on the next start up.

We use this to our advantage and make Scribe the _only_
module that loads after persistence. That way, only Scribe
subscribes to the persistence services, and only the player
accomplishments are persisted between mission runs.

**Wait... haven’t I’ve seen this mission before?**
In case you were wondering: yes, this demo is 80% of
Angels of Caucasus – except that I moved it to
Batumi, used multiple CSAr zones, a randomizer and
polished some more. But this demo is the essential
prototype for many city-based rescue scenarios.


## 8.83 Civvy Heli

### 8.83.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission shows how you set up a mission to have AI helicopters randomly fly between
points on the map.

### 8.83.2 What To Explore

_8.83.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission, switch to F10 map view, and lean back – there is nothing to fly for yourself,
all you are expected to do is watch.

Zoom out slightly so that you can see all four red points marked “Upper City”, “Lower
Meadows”, “Lower City” and “Senaki Airfield”.

After a short while, you’ll notice neutral (white) helicopters spawning at two of the red circles
(these spawns are randomized, no way of telling which two points it will be, nor what the
type of the spawning helicopter will be).

Use F2 to briefly inspect the helicopters and note their type and livery. The helicopters will
take off, fly to their destination, and land there.

After a while (you may want to accelerate time) you may see the following patterns emerge

- Whenever a helicopter spawns at Senaki Airfield, it is a Huey (UH-1)
- There never spawn two Helicopter at once at Senaki Airfield
- The Huey at Senaki Airfield always starts cold, all the helicopters at other locations
    start hot
- There’s never a flight that goes TO Lower Meadows, but flights come FROM that
    location
- The only helicopter types that spawn are UH-1H, Mi-8MT and SA342L
- Helicopters can simultaneously take off and land at Senaki

##### 8.83.2.2 ME

Only Three Types
In this mission, civHelo only uses three
types of helicopter: Hueys, Hips and
Gazelles. This is controlled by the


civHeloConfig zone that limits all types that are used to these three.

**Only cold Hueys start at Senaki**
There are _two_ civHeloZones at Senaki Airfield, and only one of them (the northern one) can
spawn helicopters, while helicopters can land at both zones. All this is controlled by the
zones Senaki Heli-1 and Senaki Helo-2.

Senaki Helo- 1 is the more interesting of
these two: it tells civHelo under _types_
that only UH-1 (Hueys) may start here,
and that the helicopters that start here
should cold-start.

Senaki Helo-2 is also interesting only
because of the fact that it is very close to
Senaki Helo-1, and does not allow
helicopters to depart from here.

This means that there can only ever depart one Helicopter at the same time from Senaki (a
Huey from Senaki Helo-1), while it is quite possible that two helicopters can be arriving at
Senaki Airfield at the same time: one at Senaki Helo-1 and one at Senaki Helo-2. Since both
zones are very close (less than minDist apart) together, civHelo will never generate a flight
between Senaki Helo-1 and Senaki Helo- 2.

Note that although we are using heli pads for all our non-airfield locations, this is not required
if the ground is flat, so you do not have to place those helipads. It looks better, though.

### 8.83.3 Discussion

CivHelo is a very easy to use module that can generate attractive local helicopter traffic for
your missions. There are some other, unexpected features that you can use:

**Challenge**
There’s an offshore oil platform off to the west. Add its helicopter platform as a departure-
only source of flights, and make only Hips (Mi-8) depart from there.

Then see what happens if you allow flights to land at that platform.


## 8.84 Red vs. Blue / Blue vs. Red (shared data)

### 8.84.1 Demonstration Goals

Some modules (e.g. “Scribe”) can share data across missions. Usually, when a mission
saves data, that data is written to a private data file (see the _persistence_ module). Some
modules can opt to instead to write their data to a different file in DML’s ‘shared data’
directory. These modules then can access that shared data and ‘continue to use’ the data,
which can result in the illusion that the mission ‘continues’ from previous missions. For
example, the “Angels” series of (freely available) rescue sandbox missions (Caucasus,
Levant) all share the player’s achievements (like number of people rescued).

This demo mission shows how the scribe module uses the ‘sharedData’ attribute to achieve
this.

### 8.84.2 What To Explore

This demo consists of two missions: “rvb BLUE” and “rvb RED”

**Note:**
In this mission we use the two free DCS aircraft Su-25T and TF-51 simply to be able to tell
them apart. It can work much better if you substitute or add your own (owned) modules for
those, but it is not required.

_8.84.2.1 Preparation_
When run, the missions access the directory “DML-Shared-Data” which by default resides in
your standard DCS Missions/ directory. If that directory “DML-Shared-Data" exists, open it,
and ensure that there is no file called “rvb.txt”. If it exists, delete it. This file holds the data
that is shared between the two “rvb” missions, and to make sure that we get the same
experience, delete the file to reset the data that is shared.


_8.84.2.2 In Mission_
Start with the BLUE side first: start “rvb BLUE”

Enter one of the planes. Since everyone has access to the free TF-51, I’ll choose “Blue
Mustang” even though I’d much more have preferred another one that is tantalizing me.

As soon as you enter the cockpit, you are greeted with

That is the _scribe_ module, duly noting your arrival, and showing you your current statistics:
zero total time, no take-offs, landings, crashes, nor start-ups.

Get oriented in the cockpit, and start the engine (if you are unfamiliar with this aircraft, use
auto-start). Once the engine is running, go to Communication→Other→Mission Logbook and
choose “Get Pilot’s Statistics”.

Your total time has increased, and you have one start credited to you. Look around some
more, then go to Communications→Other→Save Mission and choose “Save Now”.

Persistence tells you that it saved the mission.

_Optionally, on a re-run of this demo:_
also enter any other plane that is available to you in this map.

Now exit the mission, and run “rvb RED”. Note that you are running this mission for the first
time, that it has never before saved data.

Enter the Su-25T “Frogger RED”. You are greeted with something like

Note that _scribe_ greets you, and that _it includes stats for time and other data that you have
accumulated in the “rvb BLUE” mission_ : your time in the TF-51 is accounted for, even though
“rvb RED” does not even offer you to enter that aircraft! Your time and other stats for your
current aircraft (the Frogfoot) are set to zero.

Start the Frogfoot (make sure that your throttle is on idle!), make sure that scribe has
registered your start-up:


Then save the mission the same way that you did before.

Exit the mission, start “rvb BLUE” again, and enter the TF-51 once more.

Your accomplishments from the mission “rvb RED” have carried over to “rvb BLUE”: your
time and start-ups in the Frogfoot are shown even though there is no Frogfoot in this
mission.

##### 8.84.2.3 ME

The scribe modules in both missions “rvb RED” and “rvb BLUE” share their data: what it
saved from one mission is read (and saved) by the other. We accomplish this with a single
attribute in scribe’s config zone _scribeConfig_ :

The _sharedData_ attribute tells scribe
that it should save and retrieve any data
from a file called “rvb” that resides inside
the “DML-Shared-Data” folder instead of the mission’s private data folder.

Note that both missions “rvb BLUE” and “rvb RED” must have configured scribe that way in
order for both missions to share their data.

In the Image above, we see the contents of my “DML-Shared-Data” folder. There’s the file
“rvb.txt” that is shared by the missions “rvb RED” and “rvb BLUE”, and another (not
discussed here) file “Angels.txt” that is shared by other missions (my “Angels” mission series
that currently comprises of “Angels of Caucasus”, “Angels of the Levant: Syria” and “Angels
of the Levant: Sinai”).


## 8.85 Non-SSB and SP slot blocking

### 8.85.1 Demonstration Goals

The _Slotty_ module provides “SSB” smart slot blocking for single-player games, and can
serve as a fallback for multiplayer games if SSB isn’t installed on on the server. This mission
demonstrates the effects of active slot blocking of player aircraft on airfields that do not
belong to the player’s faction (the latter being provided by the _ssbClient_ module).

This demo shows in single-player how a player-slot is blocked from entering while the airfield
belongs to red, and the slot then becomes available after the airfield is captured by blue.

After that, the demo also shows how _slotty_ provides a fallback to provide support for the SSB
protocol when SSB itself is disabled or not installed.

### 8.85.2 What To Explore

_8.85.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission in **Single-Player**. Before you can choose a slot, some of the typical DML
startup diagnostics appear. Relevant for later is only one:

There are two slots available to choose from. Choose “Forbidden Frog”, the Su-25T located
at Kutaisi airfield. Note that you can choose the slot. Enter the aircraft. Press “Fly” in the
ensuing Briefing dialog.

As soon as the dialog disappears, so will the cockpit, and you are afforded a nice yet
uneventful view of Kutaisi: you have been removed from the plane because it sits on Kutaisi
airfield which is currently owned by RED and the plane that you entered is a BLUE plane.
There’s also a not to that effect:

Now press ‘ESC’ to return to slot selection, and choose “Senaki Frog”. This aircraft you can
enter, and after the briefing screen you are sitting comfortably at the controls of your
Frogfoot.

Now Choose Communications→Other→Capture Kutaisi

Wait a few seconds until you see the message

appear among some others.

Now, press ‘ESC’ again to return to slot selection and choose “Forbidden Frog” again.

This time you can enter the Frogfoot at Kutaisi.

Now start the mission as **Multiplayer**


Go through the same steps and note that the results are the same: you can’t enter the
“Forbidden Frog” until you have captured Kutaisi

If you have installed SSB on your DCS also note that server-side SSB is disabled

##### 8.85.2.2 ME

Since Slotty ans SSBClient work out-of-the-box there is very little going on in ME outside of
adding the dependend modules, and setting the owners of the airfield. Kutaisi is to be owned
initially by RED, Senaki by BLUE.

Capturing Kutaisi is done by activating a ‘late activation’ group of BLUE vehicles that are
inside Kutaisi’s capture circle.

### 8.85.3 Discussion

There is one tidbit worth mentioning
and that is when we tested multiplayer:
no matter if you have SSB installed on
your DCS, it does not kick in for this
mission. This is intentional, since we wanted to demonstrate Slotty’s ability to work its magic
in multiplayer as well. This is accomplished by SSBClient’s config zone, which has set the
ssbAutoenable attribute to false. This prevents sscClient from enabling SSB on the server,
so it does not know that the mission is using the SSB protocol, and will not interfere.


## 8.86 What’s on the cascading menu

### 8.86.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission demonstrates how you use trigger zones with ‘radioMenu’ und ‘radioMainMenu’
attributes to create “cascading” menus, i.e. menus in menus.

### 8.86.2 What To Explore

_8.86.2.1 In Mission_
There is no flying in this demo. Simply enter the Frogfoot and go to “Communications→F10
Other”. Note the menu options:

First, choose “Top Level Stuff”, then choose “Previous Menu” and choose "Level 1 Menu”

First choose “Mid Level Stuff”, then “Previous Menu” and choose “Leven TWO menu”

Finally, choose “Bottom Level (2) Stuff”

As you can see from the leading Numbers in the upper left corner we are progressing further
down the menu tree.

The entire tree was constructed with DML-enhanced trigger zones that use either a
_radioMainMenu_ or _radioMenu_ attribute.

##### 8.86.2.2 ME

So let’s look at this in Mission Editor. There are a total of 5 trigger zones that are used to
assemble this (complex) menu tree:


There are three radioMenu (orange) trigger zones that provide their menu and items at the
various levels, and two (green) radioMainMenu trigger zones that provide sub-menus where
radio menus can plug into. Each radioMenu plugs into a radioMenu with an ‘attachTo:’
attribute, or (when there is not ‘attachTo:’ attribute) directly appears inside the F-10 Other
menu.

### 8.86.3 Discussion

Remember tat the _attachTo:_ attribute always refers to the _Trigger Zone Name_.

**Don’t become cyclic**
It’s difficult to do so, but people have tried: You can create cyclic ‘cyclic’ menu structures
with “radioMainMenu” by referring from A to B and then in B referring back to A (or in more
complex structure). Most often, when this happens, it appears when you are restructuring
your mission. DML detects such an issue, and breaks a cycle, issuing a warning similar to

DML lists all trigger zones by names that are part of at least one cycle, and you should
check these zones and break all cycles. Note that all menus that are part of that cycle will
not work. Note also that cycles in your menu structure will not cause DML to freeze.


**Challenge:**
In the demo mission there are two trigger zones called “Cyclic A” and Cyclic B*. Their
radioMainMenu attributes have been rendered inert by an inserted X.

To see how DML handles cyclic structures, remove the “X” from both, and run the mission.
You’ll get the error shown above.

Now break the cycle, and insert Cyclic A and Cyclic B before “Level 2”.


## 8.87 Reaper, man

### 8.87.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission shows you how you can set up reaper drones to use with your missions – and
use the built-in interface as well as a flag/radioMenu-driven ‘homebrew’ interface.

### 8.87.2 What To Explore

_8.87.2.1 In Mission_
**Note:**
To test target lasing provided by drones, you need a module that supports laser-guided
munitions. This demo mission provides slots for the F-16 and F-5E. You may want to
exchange this for your preferred LGB bomb truck.

Enter your trusty “The Watcher” (Su-25T) cockpit at Senaki Kolkhi.

Almost immediately, a message pops up:

Switch to F-10 Map, and note that there are 4 (four) drones in
the air, looking for targets. The one in the North, East of
Sukhumi is even tracking a target, the others are idling.

(Note: don’t wait too long after entering the Frogfoot’s cockpit,
SAMs, especially SA-11 do not like to be spied on).

After a short while, you’ll receive another message:

Pan your map, and verify that you can see the target that the Zugdidi drone is tracking. Note
that red smoke erupts from the unit’s location as soon as the drone has spotted the AAV7
and starts lasing. Note also that this drone is doing its rounds at 4000m altitude.

Now, if you have the module and skills, jump into your favorite LGB bomb truck, and drop a
bomb on the hapless AAV7 that the drone is lasing. For easier orientation, look for the red
smoke.

Now go to Communication→Other→Drone Command and choose “Drone Status”. A report
appears:


Note that there are 4 drones in the air, two of them track (and lase) targets, and one drone is
ready to launch. Let’s launch it.

Go to Communications→Other→Drone Command→Launch Drones and choose
“unlaunched: MQ-9 Reaper”.

Switch back to F10 Map view and note that a new drone is circling the skies above Zugdidi,
slightly Northeast to the one that targeted the AAV7, at 9500m altitude.

At the 3 - minute mark, the SA- 11 have enough of being spied upon by a drone, and they fire
at the drone:

however, this is immediately followed up by another

and looking at the map you’ll see that the drone over the SA-11 site was immediately
replaced. If you let the mission run, this pattern repeats: the SAM site downs the drone after
a while, and a new drone arrives within a second.

Now let’s turn our eyes to the Communication→Other→Secret Drone menu and choose
“Status”

You receive a status of a drone that was not listed with the “Drone Status” command from
the “Drone Command” menu. Let’s launch it with Communications→Other→Secret
Drone→Launch:

If you switch to the F-10 Map, you’ll notice a new reaper drove circling above Batumi.

##### 8.87.2.2 ME

This demo mission shows you how you can quickly add drones to your missions and runs
through the most common use cases.

**Placing a drone on the map**
Look at the trigger zone “Kutaisi”. It’s the
most basic reaper setup: a single _reaper_
attribute with no value.


This sets up a drone using all defaults: an MQ-9 Reaper, launched at mission start, orbiting
over the trigger zone at 9’400m (28’000 ft) using a laser code of 1688. Note that this trigger
sone is quad-based.

Slightly to the west we find the trigger
zone “Honi”, which is similarly simple,
but holds the value “Predator” for the
_reaper_ attribute. This zoner spawns an RQ-1A Predator upon mission launch, which orbits
the trigger zone at 7’500m (22’500ft).

As you probably have guessed by now,
the self-respawning reaper drone
monitoring the SA-11 site is similarly
simply, and merely has an active
_autoRespawn_ attribute.

The trigger zone “Zugdidi” is more
interesting: It spawns an MQ-9 Reaper
(default) at 4000m, as defined with the
_alt_ attribute. Since visual range of
drones depends on their altitude, this
drone only has a visual range of roughly
4 km, which explains why it takes so
long to detect the AAV-7. More importantly, though, this reaper zone is set up to give pilots
more visual aids when the drone starts targeting units: it places a red ( _smokeColor = red_ )
colored smoke ( _doSmoke = yes_ ) at the point where it first spotted the unit (note that the unit
may move after being spotted. While the laser will remain on the unit, the smoke marker
remains at the initial location).

Finally, there’s the “unlaunched” trigger
zone which simply holds a drone ready
for launch, but does not launch the
drone on mission start ( _onStart = no_ ).

All of these drones can be controlled with the “Drone Command” UI that the reaper module
provides (for each side)

Note that all these drones can be launched with the Drone Command UI if they are not
loitering/tracking targets. Also (not shown with this demo), if the bank module is present and
this ability is enabled, the Drone Command UI automatically manages funds for drone
launches, and only allows drone launches if there are sufficient funds available.

### 8.87.3 Discussion

Sometimes, you may have more than 10
drones for a side, or you have drones
that you do not want to be visible from
the Drone Command UI. The reaper
zone “Secret Batumi” is one such drone.

Its _launchUI = no_ and _statusUI = no_
attributes prevent it from appearing in the
Drone Command UI. To still be able to
launch or get a status for drones
controlled by this reaper zone, we have the inputs _status?_ and _launch?_ that allow us to


inquire the status of the drone, and launch it on-demand (if it’s not already launched). Since
it is set to _onStart = no_ , the drone does not launch on mission start.

To do this, this demo sports a
radioMenu that supplies the inputs to get
a status update and launch the drone.


## 8.88 Escort a convoy to town (Convoy & twn)

### 8.88.1 Demonstration Goals

This demo illustrates the use of the “Convoy” and “twn” modules to build convoys (Convoy)
that are randomized and move purposefully from A to B – and have improved reporting
(twn).

### 8.88.2 What To Explore

_8.88.2.1 In Mission_

**Note:**
Due to the heavy randomization built into the Convoy module, your experience of the play-
through of the demo may diverge significantly from my description. Perhaps run through the
demo a couple of times to understand what happens.

Start the mission in in the BLUE Frogfoot “Rubber Duck” and switch to F10 Map view. Go to
Communications→Other→Convoy Control→Spawn Convoys and choose “Start new BLUE
Convoy A”.

Southwest of Senaki Kolkhi a new blue convoy consisting of 3 BTR- 80 spawns. It may
spawn with a helicopter escort (this is randomized). If the convoy spawns with a helicopter
escort, quit the mission and restart until it spawns without.

After the convoy spawns, you see a message

Followed quickly by a second message

Sit back and watch the convoy proceed. You may want to accelerate time (to, say 20x) until
it reaches the road’s T-junction and turns east towards the two T- 55.


When it reaches the T-junction, a message appears

Soon after the convoy heads east, it comes into range of the two T-55 and they start
engaging the convoy:

The BTR-80a are no match for the T-55; a bit later

##### .

Followed by a message that there now a 0 blue convoys.

Now launch the same convoy again, this time _with_ a helicopter escort. The two Cobras make
short work of the two T- 55. Yes, helicopter escorts can be a nasty piece of work, so be
careful when you assign them in your mission as not to cut through too many defenses...

Since it is unopposed, this time the convoys makes it past all its checkpoints

While the convoy is moving, and close to Senaki Kolkhi, go to
Communications→Other→Convoys and choose “List known Convoys”.

When it arrives at Senaki-Kolkhi, or more precisely, at the point 2.1 km SE of Teklati

At that point in the mission, the convoy (vehicles and helicopters vanish)

Now, go to Communications→Other→Convoy Control→Spawn Convoys and choose “Start
RED Coastal Convoy”. A RED convoy starts (consisting of either two or 5 vehicles), escorted
by helicopter(s): either a Huey or two Cobras. If you are sill in the BLUE “Rubber Duck”
Frogfoot, a message appears

Call up the “List known Convoys” report

Side note: Remember that “nasty piece of work” bit? If the red convoy that you spawned is
escorted by Cobras and you sit on Senaki-Kolkhi’s tarmac in a blue Frogfoot, there is a good
chance that one of the Cobras decides that it doesn’t like you and attempts to kill you.

After a (rather long) while, it arrives in Poti and disappears:

Lastly, go to Communications→Other→Locator and choose “Tell me where I am”:


##### 8.88.2.2 ME

**Named Map Locations**
Let’s begin with the easiest first, as it
also serves well to describe what the twn
module does behind the scenes: When
the player invokes the “Tell me where I
am command”. This simply triggers the
messenger in the “Locateme” zone:

As you can see, it’s a simple compound
zone, stacking a radioMenu to trigger a flag where, and a messenger that triggers on the
where flag.

When the messenger is triggered, it outputs the message “I am <loc:Locateme>.”. The
interesting bit here is the wildcard <loc:Locateme> which uses the twn module to translate a
point into a map location. “Locateme” is a trigger zone, so
the center of the trigger zone is used as reference for the
location.

The trigger zone is north of a village called Abasha on the
map. Twn retrieves the position and name of the closest
map locale, measures the distance, and then creates an
appropriate message:

Outside of wildcards, this ability of twn to retrieve map locations becomes automatically
available to other modules, and all that mission designers must do to access it is to add the
twn module to the mission.

**Convoys**
Let’s look at red convoy that we spawned last,
as it looks deceptively simple, yet packs a lot of
cool features. Let us inspect this convoy’s setup
first:

If you look closely, you’ll see that there are two
groups of ground vehicles inside this zone: a
blue (three units) and a red (five units) group.
This is to demonstrate multiple features:

```
a) that Convoy doesn’t care what side a
convoy belongs to when a group is defined. When the convoy is spawned, ownership
is determined by the zone’s coalition attribute (which is red)
b) You can place multiple groups inside a convoy zone (and only one unit has to
actually be inside the trigger zone). When a convoy is launched, one of the groups is
chosen by random
```

There are two helicopter groups inside the “Durgena Red Supply” trigger zone: Like with the
ground vehicles, you can add multiple helicopter groups to a convoy zone, and when the
convoy spawns, one of the possible escort groups is chosen at random.

The convoy zone itself holds no surprises:

_coalition_ is set to red (which makes all
convoys started by this zone members
of RED coalition), irrespective of which
coalition the groups belonged to when
the groups were set up in Mission
Editor.

The convoy spawns when the flag _goB_ that is connected to the _spawn?_ input changes. All
other attributes are using their default values, meaning that – since there are helicopter
groups defined – there is always a helicopter escort (pEscort defaults to 100%) and that no
convoy spawns at mission start.

Another perhaps interesting aspect: although one of the helicopter groups has a (simple)
route defined, that is ignored and the helicopters escort the convoy, ignoring any previously
defined routes.

If we now turn our attention to the blue
convoy, we see that its setup is more
simple: a single group of vehicles, and a
single helicopter group. However, the
convoy’s pEscort attribute is set to 50,
meaning that only in 50% of all spawns
the convoy should receive a helicopter
escort. This is the reason that you may
have had to try multiple times for the escort to start un-escorted. We also see here that the
_onStart_ attribute is set to false – something that isn’t necessary (since it defaults to false) but
something I like to do for some zones to be more explicit should I later want to maintain the
mission.

### 8.88.3 Discussion

**On the Menu**
There is a bit more depth to the convoy
module that we want to explore in this
section. Convoy comes with its own
built-in GUI, and when enabled usually
installs itself at the
Communications→Other level. Here we use the config zone’s attachTo: attribute to attach
Convoy’s convoy status menu to a Main Menu named “Convoy Main Menu” provided by
radioMenu. That radio menu provides further convoy options, like launching the various
convoys.


**Counting blue Convoys**
There’s another attribute in Convoy’s config: _blueConvoy#_. This is a direct output that
connects to the flag named _blueCNum_ and always is updated to the number of currently
running blue convoys. Mission designers can use to trigger many effects, here we merely
use it to trigger a messenger that always updates us when the number of running blue
convoys changes:

Again, few surprises: the messenger
triggers every time that the value of the
flag blueCNum changes. Since
blueCNum is fed directly from Convoy’s
‘living blue convoy’ counter, this messenger trigger whenever a new blue convoy is created
or a blue convoy is destroyed (note that this can lead to an unlikely but still possible side
effect: if within a short time a blue convoy is created and destroyed, the net result on the
direct output may not change, and the messenger nit trigger. This unlikely even becomes
very likely indeed if you feed a convoy’s “ _dead!_ ” output into its “ _spawn?_ ” input to create ever-
running convoys. A side-effect that you need to be aware of if you use Convoy’s direct
outputs to monitor the flow of convoys in your mission)

**Challenges:**

**Where am I?**
Let’s start simple. The messenger that tells you where I am is set up inefficiently: it hogs the
flag named ‘where’. Change this so that, as a true stacked module, it uses a local flag.

And now let’s make this stack more useful: use it to always tell us the location of the unit
“Rubber Duck”.

**So close!**
When you start the red convoy and request a report, it always screams at everyone on
BLUE that it is CLOSE TO DESTINATION!!!!one

Find out why, and change the set up so that this warning only comes up when the convoy is
within 2 miles of Poti. Remember that there are two possible groups of convoys.


## 8.89 The Yeller Model

### 8.89.1 Demonstration Goals

This miz functions as a hands-on example for the “Yeller Model” explanation introduced
early in this doc: it’s a simplified, yet highly effective way to understand how how modules
talk to each other, and it completely avoids “Flags”. Please read the relevant chapter, as it
(hopefully) makes understanding a central aspect of DML much more accessible.

### 8.89.2 What To Explore

_8.89.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission and enter the Frogfoot.

A message “Time to go now” appears on-screen.

Wait.

Every 90 seconds, another message “Time to go” appears.

Get bored, end mission.

##### 8.89.2.2 ME

### 8.89.3 Discussion

As per “Yeller Model”, we use different modules: some yell commands, others listen for
commands, some to both. In this
mission, we are using the Yeller module
pulseZone, which sends the “gogogo”
command (or “yells gogogo”) over its
“pulse!” output (in classic “Flag Model”,
the module changes the value of the Flag named “gogogo”).


As we can see, the pulser is set up to send the “gogogo” command every 90 seconds (time
= 90) on the pulse! output.

The Listener module is a messenger
that waits for the command “gogogo”.
When it hears the command, it outputs
the message “Time to go now” to the
screen.

As you can see, using the “Yeller Model” can dramatically help to understand how the
various modules talk to each other without losing significant context. It’s up to you to take the
simplified approach (“commands” versus “signals on flags”) – as long as it works for you.


## 8.90 Inferno at Sea (heloTroops: dropZone)

### 8.90.1 Demonstration Goals

HeloTroops, due to its ability to allow players to pick up and transports infantry from
anywhere to everywhere on the map, has proven to be one of the most successful and fun
modules. Even though it was conceived as a drop-in module, mission designers can use
additional built-in functionality to fine-tune its operation. This mission high-lights heloTroop’s
dropZones – it’s mechanism to restrict troop delivery to certain places, and send signals
(commands) when players deliver units to

(IMG)

In this demo we use a dropZone to restrict all troop deployment from helicopters to the
“Return Here” zone on Batumi’s Tarmac close to the tower:

### 8.90.2 What To Explore

_8.90.2.1 In Mission_

**Note** :
This mission requires that you fly a helicopter well enough to land on an off-shore oil
platform, and return that helicopter back to Batumi and land inside a defined area,

**Note**
This mission requires a troop-transport capable helicopter: Huey, Hip, Hind or Hook. The
mission uses a Huey, you can simply add one of the other helicopters and the mission will
work

Start in the Huey and fly to the burning platform west of Batumi. Land on the Platform.

The three infantry units board the helicopter automatically

Take off and return to Batumi, and land on the runway. Note that no units disembark.

Now land on the tarmac next to the Tower (look for 4 Hueys and firetrucks) where you
initially started.


As soon as you touch down, the infantry disembarks, and you see a message
“Congratulations – You have saved 1 groups of evacuees” (or similar).

60 seconds later, the units that disembarked your helicopter disappear.

Note that at no point in time was there any player interaction required for the units to hop on
or off the helicopter.

##### 8.90.2.2 ME

This simple demo entirely relies on heloTroops’ dropZone abilities:there is a dropZone at
Batumi’s tarmac with two attributes:

The dropZone! Attribute itself that sends
a signal on the flag _saved_ connected to
the output dropZone! (“yells the
command _saved_ ”) whenever an eligible
player helicopter lands inside and disembarks troops.

The disembarked troops are automatically de-spawned after 60 seconds ( _autoDespawn_ =
60)

The mission’s behavior is rounded off by some settings inside the configZone:

- most importantly,
    _enforceDropZones_ is set to true,
    which prevents troops to
    disembark from player
    helicopters when the helicopter in
    on the ground. We saw this when we touched down on Batumi’s runway and tried to
    unload our troops via F-10 Other.
- We also set troop pick-up and drop-off to automatic. This is mere mission polishing,
    as users can override this setting with heloTroops natice UI. This also demonstrates
    nicely that players cannot override the _enforceDropZones_ setting manually.

### 8.90.3 Discussion / Challenges

There are some hidden niceties in this mission:

- The spawner on the oil platform spawns troops a minute after a helicopter has picked
    up the last batch. This is standard spawner behavior and works around a possible
    issue if you set the spawner to only spawn when the last batch was delivered to
    Batumi, and the helicopter crashed with units on board, or accidentally killed all units
    on the platform. This also allows multiplayer gameplay.
- The messenger counts the number of deliveries
- The fires have been put at an altitude to make them appear to come from within the
    platform


**Challenges:**

- Change the spawners setup so that units do not spawn automatically (i.e. set the
    spawner’s paused attribute to true). Then figure out a safe way how you can spawn
    evacuees on the platform so that the mission is multiplayer-capable and can handle
    the case when a helicopter loaded with troops crashes and is lost.
- Have the messenger count the number of units saved instead of the number of
    groups.


## 8.91 Usher me to my plane

### 8.91.1 Demonstration Goals

This demo aims to show how you can use the _usher_ module to generate signals/commands
when players connect for the first time, or the number of players in your mission changes.

### 8.91.2 What To Explore

Due to the way that DCS works, this mission can show different (intermediary) results
depending on if it’s run as single-player or in multiplayer mode.

_8.91.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission in _single-player_ mode.

Choose blue coalition, and enter the (free) TF-51D

As soon as you enter the mission (while the briefing is still being displayed), two messages
appear:

and

Shortly thereafter (once you fully enter the cockpit and the simulation starts), three more
messages appear:


Now press “ESC” and use CHOOSE SLOT to enter (free) SU-25T.

Two messages come up

Now press ESC, and CHOOSE COALITION to switch to red, then enter the red Frogfoot at
Kutaisi

Two more messages come up:

Now re-run the same mission, but this time, run the mission as **multiplayer**.

Note that essentially, you get the same
notification, but that there are additional
messages in-between caused by the
fact that the mission in multiplayer does
not pause when a player changes slots
and/or sides. In those cases, the player
can leave the mission altogether and
return for the same or different side,
and usher can generate events the
appropriate events (e.g. leave and join
events, multiple updates to the number
of total players, etc.). The number of
messages that appear can be different
depending on how quickly a player
changes from one plane to another.

For example, this is how it could look
when the player changes from the blue
TF-51 to the blue Su-25T.

Note the multiple “There are now X
blue players in-game”, and the notice
that “A player has left” and “A player
has joined”. This is because **in
multiplayer** , when a player changes an
aircraft, they first completely leave the game before they re-enter after they have chosen a
new aircraft. **Single-player** may behave similarly, but since the mission is paused during re-
slotting, usher isn’t invoked and therefore can’t notice any changes. The mission continues
to run in multiplayer, and usher notes these changes - and creates signals/commands
accordingly.


##### 8.91.2.2 ME

Usher knows no other trigger zones other than the usherConfig configuration zone, and that
is where we will start:

- Since playerEvent defaults to
    true, we merely change the
    message that every player
    receives when they first connect
    to the mission to “<n>Welcome,
    <p> to your <c> <t>!
    Let's kick <e>'s
    butt!<n>.” This results in the
    personalized message that each
    and every player receives when
    they first connect to the mission.
    Note the use of various wildcards
    like <e> and <p>.
- We also output the current number of red players to the flag named “redNum” via the
    direct output “red#”, which allows us to trigger other modules every time that there is
    a change
- We do the same via “blue#” which connects to “blueNum”
- And again, for the total number of players inside the mission via direct output
    “pNum#” which connects to “total”.
- Whenever the total number of players increases, a signal/command “join” is sent
- And likewise whenever the total number of players decreases, a signal/command
    “leave” is send
- We also turn on typeEvents (these are special events that create signals based on
    the type of the plane that a player enters)
- We set typeMsg to empty, which will suppress a message the first time that a player
    enters a new type of aircraft.
- And we set ups to 1 (1 update per second), a tenfold increase over the default, used
    here only because I hate to wait during testing. The default (0.1) is a much better
    value, and one that I recommend for real missions.

This setup drives all the action and all of the messenger zones.

The three trigger zones “blue Players”,
“red Players” and “total Players” all use
the same method: They trigger on a
change in the direct outputs “blue#”,
“red#” and “pNum#”. Whenever the number in the direct output changes, the messenger
triggers, and puts out the message, using wildcards to create relevant information.

The messengers for “player Join” and “player Leave” function the same way: triggered by a
change in the output, displaying a wildcarded message.

The trigger zone “Su-25T entered” works
similarly, and is a bit different in one
important aspect – it is triggered by a
“special event”, a command “u:SU-25T”.
If you look closely at the mission, you will find that no trigger zone is explicitly sending this
command/signal (i.e. there is no flag name given to any output to change a flag of that


name). This is what happens when any player enters an aircraft of the type “SU-25T” for the
first time: usher takes the type of the plane, prepends “u:” to that name, and increases the
value of the flag of that name by one (sends a command “u:SU-25T”). Since we know all the
aircraft types that we (the mission designer) put into the mission, we can predict what the
commands are going to be, but we do not know when this will happen (also remember that
this only happens once, when a player chooses that type for the very first time since the
mission started).

### 8.91.3 Discussion

Usher serves multiple, at first blush, handlings for “unusual” events or “edge cases”:

- It provides ‘first time’ player events – the first time that any unique player connects,
    the first time any player enters a particular plane, or group, or type, or coalition.
    These first time-events are provided because missions are often structured in a way
    that they await an event when for “the first time a player does X”.
- It creates signals (commands) that that have sometimes impossible to predetermine
    or forge names. It allows mission designers an easy tool to create very specific
    commands that handle a very specific case that would be ungainly to achieve
    otherwise, usually in connection with player action. In some cases, it even allows
    mission designers to create events based on player names that allows them to curtail
    action to specific players should they connect (an action that I discourage, but allows
    for some nearly unbreakable access control protection for specific privileged players)

##### CHALLENGES

Using usher and some other modules, create a mission where

- We receive a message when at least one red and one blue player have connected
    (note: they do not necessarily have to be connected at the same time),
- Do the same as above, but a red and a blue player must be online at the same time
- Create a message when three players have left the game one way or another, and
    observe how differently this works in multiplayer.


## 8.92 Foggy Bottoms

### 8.92.1 Demonstration Goals

This short demo explains how to use DML’s “fogger” module to dynamically ruin player’s
visibility using simple communication commands (radioMenu)

### 8.92.2 What To Explore

_8.92.2.1 In Mission_
Enter your trusty Frogfoot, waiting for you at Batumi. Take a look outside: it’s a winter
morning in Batumi; the air is brisk and clear. Let’s change that.

Call up Communications→Other→Fog Manager... and choose “Visibility 500m in 15 secs”.

Over the next 15 seconds, watch as fog quickly creeps in, and visibility drops to 500m. That
first Beriev A-50 beyond the Herc should now only be a ghost on the verge of being invisible.

Check by removing all fog through with Communications→Other→Fog Manager and
choosing “Remove all Fog”.

Finally, let’s see how bad it can get, and choose Pea Soup. Not quite London 1952 bad, but
still a big challenge for anyone who’d attempt a landing. IMG all the way. Also, note the
message

##### 8.92.2.2 ME

There’s very little surprise in the mission: a radioMenu driving all the fog changes through
commands.

The ‘clear fog’ setting in the “Fog OFF”
(yeah, needed that) trigger zone may
surprise at first, because a _visibility_ of 0
sound like “full on”. In DCS, however,
few things are ever fully logical, and a
_visibility_ or _thickness_ of 0 turns off fog
altogether. _Duration_ 5 gives DCS five seconds for the transition


The visibility 5km zone shows a small
technical finesse that is sadly not visible
and is only included to demonstrate that
the values do support randomization:
visibility is set to randomize from 5000
to 5500 meters. Few eyes are good
enough to tell the difference in-game,
but at least you can see this feature in action.

And finally, pea soup merely reminds you that you can turn on verbosity for most trigger
zones selectively.

### 8.92.3 Discussion

The fog module holds few surprises (outside of silly conventions like “No visibility means full
visibility), so let’s kick it up a notch:

**Challenge**
To tie together the Usher and Fog modules, here’s a challenge for you:

- Create a clear-sky multiplayer mission where fog starts rolling in when the first player
    enters a Frogfoot.


## 8.93 The Smoke is ON

### 8.93.1 Demonstration Goals

This demo explains how to use the ‘smoke trial’ module smoking in missions,

**NOTE**
Currently (as of May 2023) the smoke trail ability is **badly bugged in DCS** , and ED have
been made aware of, and acknowledged this:

The smoke disappears should you descend below the altitude that you activated the smoke
at.

No schedule for fixing this feature-breaking bug has been announced. After more than 2
years, hopes are diminishing that it ever will.

### 8.93.2 What To Explore

_8.93.2.1 In Mission_
Enter the trusty airborne Su-25t “Airfrog” and establish a wings-level attitude, slightly gaining
altitude

Go to Communications→Other→Smoke Trails and choose Smoke ON.

Go to F2 external view notice that your Su-25T is now leaving behind trail of orange smoke

Now enter the “Groundfrog” Su-25T sitting cold at the airfield in Senki. Go to
communications and observe that there is no “Other...” menu to access the smoke trail
function.


##### 8.93.2.2 ME

We begin with global configuration:
smoking’s configZone tells the module
to set up all aircraft’s default smoke
color to be orange, and that there
should be a GUI. The latter attribute
(GUI) isn’t required since it merely affirms the default, but it may come in helpfully later.

The Airfrog resides inside a trigger zone
“smoker for the frogger”, which not only
details smoking instructions for that
aircraft, but its mere presence also **turns off global smoke trail abilities** (since the
presence of any trigger zone with the “smoking” attribute switches the smoking module to
individual player configuration).

### 8.93.3 Discussion

As reported to ED in January 2023, **there is a show-
stopping (literally!) bug in DCS** : the smoke trail function
as it can be accessed via AP shuts down smoke when the
plane descends below the altitude at which it started the
smoke. Smoke will again start when the player ascends
above the altitude where they started the smoke.

There is no reason why this bug was not removed, as it
has been known and acknowledged for 2 years, and all
analysis points to fixing this bug being trivial (simply move
the trigger altitude to 0). Let’s hope that ED get their talent
to also work on low-hanging fruit like this.

Coding against this bug is possible, and it would suck up
performance: it requires all actively smoking aircraft to
perform altitude checks multiple times per seconds to re-
start the smoke if the new altitude was below the last time
it was started. I decided to remove this fix from the code
and prefer ED to provide a zero-performance-cost better fix at the root cause.

Until ED fix this bug, remind your players to engage smoke at the lowest possible altitude
(e.g., directly after lifting off) to avoid this silly bug.

**Challenges**
The smoking module has two methods to imbue aircraft with the smoke ability: Selectively
(by putting aircraft in a smoking zone), or collectively. Here are some challenges for you see
if you have mastered both:

**Individually**
Give the Groundfrog Su-25T the ability to leave red smoke trails, while Aifrog remains being
able to leave rust-colored trails


**All**
Give all player-controlled aircraft the ability to leave smoke trails. The smoke trails should all
be blue

**None**
Even though you have added the smoking module added to the mission, turn off all player
access to it.


## 8.94 Lazie Troops [“lase” orders, heloTroops, jtacGrpUI, CA]

### 8.94.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission demonstrates a number of DML abilities “in the wild”:

- jtacGRPUI’s ability to customize reports via wildcards
- “lase” orders with different laser codes
- heloTroop’s ability to transport (player-controllable) vehicles

**Note:**
To understand the topics discussed in this section, you need to be familiar with the modules
_SpawnZones_ , _heloTroops_ , and _jtacGrpGUI_.

### 8.94.2 What To Explore

**Note:**
Parts of this demo mission require access to one of the following modules: UH-1 Huey, Mi- 8
Hip, CH-47 Hook, Mi-24 Hind as well as CA (Combined Arms)

_8.94.2.1 In Mission_
Enter the Frogfoot “Groundfrog” at Senaki-Kolkhi, and wait for a few seconds.

- Go to communications→Other and choose jtac Lasing Report

```
Note that the two JTACs use different laser codes (disregard the fact that these
aren’t “real-life” laser codes, this is just a demo.
```

Note that the report’s format is quite different from the default report that you get from
jtacGrpUI: it leads with the jtac’s lase code, and shows latitude and longitude of the lased
unit instead of range and bearing to the requesting unit.

**For the remainder of this demo, you need access to one of these modules** (but do not
require knowledge on how to operate them): UH-1 Huey, Mi-8 Hip, Mi-24 Hind, CH-47 Hook.

- Change slots
- Enter one of the helicopters, and go to communications→Other→Airlift Troops and
    choose Request <JTAC Hill>
- Note that two Soldiers (M4) and a Hummer appear (you should hear the sound of the
    Hummer) close to the heli pads.
- Briefly after spawning in, the new JTAC Hill group reports that it is lasing a target
    (AAV7)
- Note that you can take command of the Hummer (if you own CA) by changing into
    the JTAC slot and choosing the M1025)
- Go to Communications→Other→Airlift Troops and choose <Load JTAC-Hill 1>. Both
    infantry soldier and the Hummer disappear. They have been loaded into the
    helicopter (note: this may feel strange for the Huey, as it can’t carry that much
    weight. Since it’s a computer program: magic!)
- You _could_ now fly the helicopter to some other place, but being lazy, we simply wait a
    few seconds, and then go to Communications→Other→Airlift Troops and choose
    Deploy Team <JTAC Hill-1>
- The team deploys around the Helicopter, and you’ll hear the sound of the Hummer’s
    engine again. Soon after that you’ll see the message coming up that the team is
    again lasing the AAV7.
- Now change slot to the JTAC/Operator position, and enter the Hummer (M1025) that
    was just unloaded from the Helicopter
- Go to Communications→Other and choose jtac Lasing Report

```
Yeah, you are JTAC-Hill 1
```

##### 8.94.2.2 ME

Here are the settings that allow mission designers to do similar things:

**The lasing Report**
We use jtacGrpUIConfig to create a new
report format. It heavily uses the wide
array of wildcards that DML/jtacGrpUI
supports. Remember that for Who, the
referenced unit is the lasing unit, and for What, the referenced unit is the targeted unit. Both
Who and What have access to the player group that is receiving the report:

- _who_ : <g>:code <code> -- fetches the lasing group’s name, adds a colon “:” and
    the laser code used for targeting
- _what_ : , lasing <typ> [<lat>:<lon>] – adds a “,”, some space and the word
    “lasing”, then the type of the targeted unit and puts the coordinates of the targeted
    unit in latitude and longitude into square backets “[]”, separated by a colon “:”

**Group-individual lase codes**
This is only new for you if you came to
DML before version 2.4 (early 2025).
Spawners now support a _code_ attribute
that, when combined with “lase” _orders_ ,
sets the lase code for the entire group.
If you do not provide a lase code, the
spawned group uses the default laze
code from groundTroops.

Here, code is set to “7777” to demonstrate that DML is happy to accept ‘illegal’ lase codes
that may, or may not be supported by the laser-homing device inside the game, and it will
not generate an error if use illegal codes.

**Packing and driving the Hummer**
Usually, heloTroops disallows players
to transport anything but a selection of
infantry troops. However, since
heloTroops does not enforce weight
limits, you can tell it to also allow other units to ‘sit’ in the back cabin. Here I tell
heloTroopsConfig to allow the standard Soldier M4 and Hummer to be transported with
helicopters (and nothing else). This looks and feels silly when used with smaller helicopters
(like the Huey) or really big vehicles, but if you allow it with the config zone, DML obeys.


**Driving the unpacked Hummer**
This merely calls to attention another upgrade to DML’s abilities in early 2025: when you
spawn a “ _drivable:true_ ” (player can control via Combined Arms) unit, and that unit can be
transported with a helicopter, heloTroops preserves this ability and players can also control
that unit after a helicopter transports it. In our demo, we apply this to the Hummer to show
how useful can be.


## 8.95 Yes you CAn score (CA and PlayerScore)

**Note:**
This demo requires the _Combined Arms_ module

### 8.95.1 Demonstration Goals

This demo shows how players who control ground units via “Combined Arms” can also
receive score points using the _PlayerScore_ module.

### 8.95.2 What To Explore

_8.95.2.1 In Mission_
Enter the Frogfoot or Hog and destroy one of the vehicles. Note how your score is deferred
to after landing at Kobuleti.

Restart the mission.

Now enter the ZSU-23 (using Combined Arms) and fire a short burst at one of the BTR80s in
front of you. It’ll catch fire, and (usually) start cooking off.

Note that unless you destroy the unit outright, it starts “cooking off”, with no score being
attributes yet.

Wait until the unit explodes and note that you are being awarded some score.

Note that this score is awarded immediately, it is _not_ deferred


##### 8.95.2.2 ME

There are no additional settings required to enable this ability

### 8.95.3 Discussion

Remember that negative scores (for example killing own troops) only apply to the player, not
the player’s faction to discourage griefers.


## 8.96 Plane Guard

### 8.96.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission shows how easy it is to use the planeGuard module to add helicopters that
follow naval units at a pre-set distance and offset.

### 8.96.2 What To Explore

_8.96.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission. Once it runs, hit F9 to cycle though the various naval units. There are
three Blue, and one Red naval unit in this mission. Each time, rotate the view to inspect the
plane guard helicopters. There are two CH-53, one UH-1, and one Mi-8 operating plabe
guard duties.

While the mission starts, the various helicopters take about one minute to reach their
locations. Once they are on-station, they remain there relative to the naval unit that they
perform plane guard for, even if the units change course.

##### 8.96.2.2 ME

Look at the three blue naval units.
Each of them has their own
planeGuard trigger zone, so that each
one of them is accompanied by a
plane guard helicopter (not realistic,
but great to look at).

Note that all three naval units all
belong to the same naval group. One
of the great advantages is that DML planeGuard zones perform all calculations for placing
and following _any_ unit in a naval group (not just the first).


### 8.96.3 Discussion

Note that helicopters on plane guard never run out of fuel, they will never try to land and
refuel on the naval unit that they follow.

Note also that all helicopters that are on plane guard spawn at mission start.


## 8.97 Firestarter (Airtank, Inferno)

### 8.97.1 Demonstration Goals

This miz shows to use the “airtank” and “inferno” modules together to provide a “fire fighter
training” experience at Senaki Kolkhi and a full firefighting mission on three other locations of
the map.

**Note:**
This mission does not showcase the abilities of the “fireCtrl” module that can direct “inferno”
and “airtank” modules. A separate demonstration mission is planned

### 8.97.2 What To Explore

**Note:**
To play this demo it is required that you a) have access to one of the fire-fighting helicopter
modules, and b) be able to fly helicopters well enough to perform the tasks required of you.
Be advised that this mission isn’t suitable for rotor-wing neophytes.

_8.97.2.1 In Mission_
Start the mission and take note (i.e. read if you aren’t familiar with how firefighting works in
DML) of the briefing text.

- Enter your favorite “Training” helicopter (I’m using the Huey).
- Take a look around and note the white smoke. Return here to refill your helicopter’s
    water tanks
- In fact, do it right now: go to Communications→Other→Firefighting and choose
    Activate/Ready intake. A second later, your helicopter’s tanks are filled, and it is
    markedly heavier.
- While you do that, not the fire that is erupting to the East
- Take off, head to the fire, and drop water into the flames until they go out. You drop
    water by going to Communications→Other→Firefighting and choosing MANUAL
    RELEASE


- Note that blue smoke on the ground marks the spot where the water hit the ground.
- Once the fire is out, note that you are being credited for helping to douse the fire
- You now know how to extinguish fires
- Go to F-10 map and look to the west to the red arrow close to “Camp Smokeybear”.
- Change slot to your favorite non-training helicopter. I’ll again be taking the Huey.
- Look around and note the white smoke and blazing forest to the East.

```
The white smoke again tells us where we can top up our tanks, but we now use the
real-men version: take off, turn on your intakes in mid-flight, and hover above the
river until the helicopter’s tanks are full. Be careful not to be pulled into the water.
```
- Extinguish the blaze using multiple trips to refill your tanks.
- Now look at the f-10 map and locate the western-most red arrow. There’s a small
    island in the river that is also ablaze.
- Note that the fire has spread across the entire island, but has not spread across the
    water.


- There are two AAV-7A1 near this location: one near the river, and one more in-land
    in Sachochuo. Select the inland-AAV and go to F-7 Unit view.
- Zoom out a bit and look at the houses nearby.
- Go to Communications→Other→Ignite and choose ‘Burn down Industry’.
- A small fire erupts in the center of the complex that quickly spreads and grows until
    the entire complex is ablaze.
- Go to Communications→Other→Ignite and choose ‘Flame out Industry’. Watch as
    the flames die out quickly, leaving smoke, and quickly everything is back to normal
    (in other words: sadly we can’t yet model burnt ground in DCS).

##### 8.97.2.2 ME

_Setting Up Camp: Airtank_

The fire-fighting basecamp that allows
helicopters to instantaneously (re-)fill
their water tanks is simply a trigger
zone with the ‘airtank’ attribute.

In this mission I merely added some eye candy and
a white smokeZone to mark that spot visually for
pilots. I recommend that you use similar effects or
objects to clearly mark these areas for your players.

Note that you do not need an airtank zone in your
mission to allow fire-fighting operations, just
including the module suffices. Putting player
helicopters into an airtank zone as staring slots
merely adds a nice touch, as they can easily top off
their tanks directly after slotting in.

I recommend that you configure player helicopters to start with 50% fuel at most, since some
helicopters cannot take off with full fuel and full water tanks.

_The “Training Fire”_

This fire sports a few unusual, well,
attributes, so let’s go through them in an
order different than displayed, just to
keep you on your toes.

First, of course, it starts burning when
the mission starts up by having _onStart_
= true. Additionally, it can be turned on
and off at will by using _ignite?_ and
_douse?_ – no surprises here. The first
unusual setting is setting _canspread_ to
false, locking this fire into a single fire
cell (which is what we want for a
controlled training fire – no matter how long it burns, it won’t spread to neighboring cells.
This can be useful in some other missions where you might want to have a single flame cell).


Turning _rndLoc_ on slightly randomizes the center of the fire inside the fire cell. This becomes
more important when there are multiple fire cells active in the same inferno, we merely
turned it on here just to show that we can.

Setting _cellsize_ to 100 (which is the default) is only here to allow you to play around with it. A
DCS fire effect is currently roughly 100m in size, so making the cells smaller makes little
sense, as they would bunch into each other. Note that you can’t currently control the size of
fire effects, and fire effects are 2D sprites (“billboards”) that always face the viewer, so it
occupies a circle of 50m radius. This may (hopefully) change in future releases.

The _impactSmoke_ attribute is a setting that you should only use for training missions. It
places blue smoke on the ground where the (dynamic) calculations for water release would
‘hit’ the ground. It’s well suited for training missions, but you should avoid it for ‘real’
missions.

(also of note: the experimental “ _markCell_ ” attribute that is no longer supported).

_The Forest Inferno_

This is a standard ‘raging inferno’ setup for a fire
that spreads inside the zone and is randomized to
be new and challenging every mission.

The fire starts automatically at mission start
( _onStart = true_ ), it spreads from the starting cell
( _canSpread = true_ ) and it can spread to 10 other
cells (if there are enough cells around inside the
inferno zone), making the inferno inside that zone consist of at most 10 fire cells.

Since _rndLoc_ is set to true, the center of each fire effect (the visual part) is randomized
inside each cell (which is 100mx100m in size, as defined by _cellSize_ ). Here it is very
important that _rndLoc_ is set to true, else the inferno would consist of up to 10 fire cells that
have their fire effect centered exactly inside their cells, making it look like a chess board
filled with flames – the human eye is exceedingly good at recognizing patterns, and this will
look unnatural, so _rndLoc_ makes the results look so much better.

_Island on Fire_

This little gem of an example shows two important
aspects of inferno zones: Since no maxSpread is
given, eventually all cells inside the zone that can
catch fire will catch fire.

More importantly, though, it shows that inferno’s
internal spread algorithm is smart enough not to
burn on water: even though the zone clearly is
large enough to have cells that consist of water
(river), it will not start a fire (spread to) there, so
the island, when fully ablaze will look as if it was
shaped by the surrounding water. That’s
because it _is_ shaped by the surrounding water
and looks neat; if your computer is suffering from
too much performance, burning islands like this can be a nice decorative backdrop.


### 8.97.3 Discussion

Let’s have some fiery fun. Change the big forest inferno so that

- It starts in the middle and then burns center-out in a ring until it is done
- Make the fires burn out faster
- Chess board fire: make the fire in the forest look like a chess board on fire


## 8.98 SAR and CSAR, sitting in a tree (csarFX)

### 8.98.1 Demonstration Goals

This mission demonstrates the use of csarFX in conjunction with csarManager and
autoCSAR to

- Adorn SAR missions with additional ‘scenery’ like fires, wrecks and collided vehicles
    and
- Turn a peaceful SAR into a contentious CSAR by placing and controlling enemies
    around the evacuee.
- Have pilots ejecting from aircraft trigger CSAR zones

This mission also shows off the advantages of using the _names_ and _twn_ modules. Why?
Because I can.

#### 8.98.2 What To Explore

**Note:**
SAR and CSAR missions are helicopter missions. This demo is designed without the
requirements of flying a helicopter. If you can, however, this demo is much more fun. And
difficult. And whatever happens, don’t ever think of entering the zone “AutoCSAR” in a
helicopter.

_8.98.2.1 In Mission_
**Note:**
Due to heavy randomization supported by csarManager and csarFX, you may get very
different results from what I show below.

- Start the mission, and jump into “Frogger” Frogfoot or (for a fun experience) the Huey
- Initially, ignore the drama unfolding inside the AutoCSAR zone
- Wait a few seconds.
- A message appears
- Change to F10 Map view
- Note the multiple blue Inf.Ge.M4 on the map, some of them close to some civilian
    objects, some close to a fire, and to the west, close to Zugdidi there are also red
    forces visible grouped around the blue symbols.


- Click on the various blue Inf.Ge.M4, press F7 (unit view) and use the mouse so
    inspect them and their surroundings. Note that some may be close to an accident, a
    fire, debris, or a lot of nothing.
- Some, in the vicinity of Zugdidi, are surrounded by enemy units.
- One blue unit is swimming in water, with only its head sticking out. Yes, there is a low
    (1%) probability that an Orca is swimming close by.
- Shift your attention to the AutoCSAR zone North of Senaki where a bunch of Hogs
    are being slaughtered by Flankers. Some of their pilots should manage to eject
    successfully. Each time one of them touches down, a CSAR mission is generated,
    with enemies close by.
- Jump into the “Hapless” frogfoot, and eject immediately. Watch your pilot’s likeness
    float gently to the ground. As soon as it touches down, it triggers a CSAR mission
    (see the enemies pop up around the touch-down site).

##### 8.98.2.2 ME

Note that csarFX adds a high degree of randomization, so your results look different from
what I show here.

Note also that after csarFX loads, it automatically integrates with csarManager’s missions.
The opposite is true as well: any mission that starts when csarManager loads does not have
any adoenments from csarFX, since that module hasn’t loaded yet (csarManager must load
before csarFX). That is the reason why in this demo all CSAR mission are started a few
seconds after all DML modules have loaded (which load AT START)

If you can fly helicopters, this missionis ready to fly. Be careful when you enter the CSAR
region: the enemies have teeth.

##### SEA SAR

This SAR mission is a straight
csarManager mission, without csarFX
attributes, yet it also can receive some
attention from csarFX: in some
missions, a boat may be close by, and
there is even a small likelihood that an
Orca whale swims in the water. If you
don’t tell your players about this, it is bound to ignite some hefty discussion whether the pilot
who saw the whale was off their meds or too deep into the drink themselves.

Note the _deferred_ attribute which is required to allow csarFX to get into csarManager’s
CSAR mission creation loop. The SAR mission is started with ethe “c” command that it
listens to on the startCSAR? input.

Note that you can control which vehicles are placed on the road through csarFX’s config
_seaTypes_ attribute.


##### LAND SAR

This is essentially the same as above
(Sea SAR), except that the evacuee is
dropped over land, with no chance of
landing in deep water or on a road.
Instead of a boat, some debris (a burnt-
out Hummer) may be placed close by.
Note again the deferred/startcSAR? combo.

Note that you can control which vehicles are placed on the road through csarFX’s config
_landTypes_ attribute.

##### ROAD ACCIDENT SAR

A road accident type of adornment is
triggered when the evacuee stands on a
road. csarManager can enforce this by
setting the onRoad attribute (part of the
standard CSAR suite), which will in turn
cause csarFX to add some dynamic
sdornments like flames, one or more
vehicles etc.

Note that you can control which vehicles are placed on the road through csarFX config’s
_roadTypes_ attribute.

##### SAR IN A CITY (BUILTUP)

When a CSAR zone has the _inBuiltup_
attribute, this signals to csarManeger and
csarFX that it must fit the evacuee into
constricted space. Therefore, there is no
space for scenery except perhaps some
smoke and/or fire.

Accordingly, there is no attribute in
csarFX available to set ‘ornamental’ objects like for road, sea or land.

##### COMBAT-SAR CSAR

As soon as the _enemies_ attribute is
filled, csarFX turns a SAR mission into a
combat mission. Note that this is only
possible with csarFX, even though it re-
purposes csarManagere’s CSAR zones.
As before, the presence of an _inBuiltup_
attribute signals that no standard
adornments/ornamental objects are
placed. _Enemies_ (and, if present,
_nasties_ ) are always placed, as any
debris (it assumed that if you


intentionally add a debris attribute over a populated area, you also want to see them), and
enemies are everywhere.

##### TRIGGERING WITH AUTOCSAR

The big CSAR zone North of Senaki can
only be triggered by pilots ejecting from
aircraft under control of the autoCSAR
module: there is no _startCSAR?_ input
and _deferred_ is true. The flock of flying
pigs in the zone will provide the ejecting
pilots, the setup of this CSAR zone can
serve in your missions (usually large-
scale multiplayer setups) for players as a
more challenging alternative for ‘deep enemy territory’ rescues as it automatically provides
hot LZs for evacuees.

Since we know that most of the planes that are downed in that area are Hogs, we limit _debris_
only to A-10As. When your Frogfoot crashes, its debris, too, will be that of an A-10A.

#### 8.98.3 Discussion

All SAR and CSAR missions start “late” (5 seconds after mission start through a standard
ME trigger rule).

This is necessary to give csarFX enough time to register with csarManager. This is
accomplished by the “deferred” attribute that is present in all CSAR zones. All CSAR
missions are then triggered when the signal “c” is sent with the FLAG INCREASE action in
the ME trigger rule.

Also, you may recall that this miz uses the _modules_ names and _twn_. Where do they come
into play? Both modules integrate automatically with other modules, and you can see them
in cation when we look at csarManager’s list of active missions (note that in order to see the
list of active csar missions you must be inside of the cockpit of a CSAR-capable helicopter –
for example the Huey):


**Challenges**

- Create a CSAR mission inside the AutoCSAR area that is not triggered by pilots
    ejecting under autoCSAR but uses the “c” command to start


### 8.99 Clone here – but not here (noSpawn zones)

#### 8.99.1 Demonstration Goals

With version 2.50, DML introduces the “noSpawn” zones for cloners that enables mission
designers to exclude areas in a clone zone from spawning if the cloner is set to spawn in
random locations.

#### 8.99.2 What To Explore

_8.99.2.1 In Mission_
Enter your trusty Frogfoot, located at Senaki-Kolkhi. Change to F10 Map view. Note the
locations of the various randomly spawned (blue) vehicles around the airfield. Note also that
none of the spawned vehicles come to spawn inside the red trigger zones as seen in ME
(above):

Note also that during mission start, a message appeared:

One if the noSpawn zone was excluded from clone zone “airport cloner” because its center
was not inside the closest clone zone that had rnd

Zoom further out until you can see the Nalchik/Baksan area. Note again that the SAM
groups are again placed randomly (this time as groups), and that none of the SAM groups
have been placed inside one of the red areas.


##### 8.99.2.2 ME

The _noSpawn_ zones are quite
straightforward in their use: place them so
that their center is inside a clone zone.

Make sure that the cloner has it’s
_rndLoc_ attribute set (otherwise it would
not require a _noSpawn_ zone and such a
cloner will ignore the _noSpawn_ zone
inside it, even if it was the closest clone
zone to the noSpawn zone), and make sure that the noSpawn zone is closest to the clone
zone that you want it to attach (again, if the cloner doesn’t have its _rndLoc_ attribute set, you
do not need to worry, as the _noSpawn_ zone ignores those cloners).

#### 8.99.3 Discussion

Use _noSpawn_ zones to ‘shape’ zones that you want to randomize. In the demo, we are using
a cloner to randomly deploy three groups of SAMs in a circular zone. However, we use
_noSpawn_ zones to ensure that the SAMs deploy outside of the center, and avoid forested
zones and the river to the east of Baksan:


### 8.100 Mission name

#### 8.100.2 What To Explore

##### 8.100.2.2 ME

#### 8.100.3 Discussion


## 9 DML FAQ

Got a question? Check here first!

### 9.1 General

#### 9.1.1 Loading Modules

- _How do I add modules?_
    There’s a video available, and it’s also explained in the chapter “Using DML” in this
    manual. In short, you add a ‘Trigger Rule’ to your mission “AT START”, and then
    paste some text into an ACTION’s text box.
- _Do I have to load DML at mission start?_
    That is the best point in time to do so. Modules assume that they load at mission
    start, and the information that they gather may not correctly reflect the current
    strategic situation if you do not.
- _Is the order in which I add modules relevant?_
    Yes, add ‘dependent’ modules before you add that module. For example, you must
    load _dcsCommon_ and _cfxZones_ before you load _messenger_. You **must** add the
    ‘ _persistence’_ module before any other module that should be able to persist data (i.e.
    my recommendation is that you load persistence directly after dcsCommon and
    cfxZones).

#### 9.1.2 Module Misbehaving?

- _My module only reacts the first time or not at all...?_
    Triggers react on change of the flag’s value. Make sure that you are _triggering_ with a
    DML flag method like ‘inc’ (one that will change the flag’s value each time) if you
    want the module to react every time. Specifically, be aware that the ‘flip’ method
    works well to guarantee a change only if one module changes that flag – if two or
    more can change the flag, if they fire at the same time, two flips in a row will reset the
    flag to its original value.
    On the other hand, your Watchflag condition may be set incorrectly, so also ensure
    that it set to ‘change’

### 9.2 Module Specific

#### 9.2.1 CloneZone

- _The group that the cloner should spawn doesn’t spawn_ ,
    even though I bang on the correct flag. When set to verbose it even claims to have
    spawned the groups, but nothing appears on the map.
    Check that you did **not check** the **Late Activation** flag in the group, as this too is
    copied to the clone, and the clone then forever waits to be activated.


- _Nothing spawns when the mission starts_
    Cloners default to removing all units in their zone (moving them to a template) and
    then do nothing. If you want a cloner to automatically clone when the mission starts,
    you must add an attribute ‘onStart’ and set its value to ‘true’

#### 9.2.2 Messenger

- _Message does not appear although it triggers correctly_
    Check the coalition attribute. It defaults to 0 (all), but if you are stacking a messenger
    with other modules on the zone (e.g., unitZone), ensure that any coalition attribute
    present uses a synonym that does not conflict with messenger.

#### 9.2.3 Object Destruct Detector

- _Module used to work, but suddenly has stopped working in my mission_
    From release to release, DCS might change the objectID of some buildings/objects
    on the map. Verify that the objectID matches the one you are looking for

#### 9.2.4 PlayerScore

- _Units do not score correctly even though they are in the Score Table_
    Since DCS 2.7, a weird quirk in the game engine can turn units that are ‘cooking off’
    (burning before they explode) into static objects. In the process they lose their
    connection to their group. As a result, PlayerScore cannot connect that unit to the
    group score. So if you award score by group name, units of that group can be
    affected by this. If this happens, PlayerScore awards the default score for that
    category instead.
- _I have set up score for scenery objects, but suddenly it is no longer awarded_
    This has the same root cause as the Object Destruct Detector issue: ED can change
    object ID for scenery objects from release to release. If you use scenery objects as
    scorable targets, you will have to check the IDs in you mission after each release.

#### 9.2.5 SSBClient

- _I have added and configured SSBClient, but it does not block any slots_
    SSBClient only works when the mission runs in multiplayer. Start it as a self-hosted
    Multiplayer game
- _Hey, I did the above, still nothing!_
    SSBClient also needs SSB installed in your Server Hooks folder. After you install
    SSB you MUST restart DCS because all server files are onle read when DCS starts
    up
- _Did all that, still no joy_
    Is the mission running? Many servers are configured so that a multiplayer mission is
    paused when it begins. Blocking slots only happens once the mission is started.


#### 9.2.6 stopGap/noGap

- _When players enter their cockpit, the plane falls to the ground, damaging it_
    Your mission is experiencing synchronization issues, a common DCS bug. Install
    ‘stopGapGUI’ or ‘noGapGUI’ on your server to work around this bug in DCS server.
